Project Page: https://github.com/FlashSampling/FlashSampling.

AI.tldr: FlashSampling is an exact categorical sampling method that fuses sampling directly into the LM-head matrix multiplication, eliminating the need to materialize logits in high-bandwidth memory by processing vocabulary tiles on-chip.
AI.motivation: Current categorical sampling in large-vocabulary language model decoding causes extra memory traffic and additional kernel calls after the LM head, creating performance bottlenecks.
AI.method: Compute logits tile-by-tile on chip, add Gumbel noise, keep one maximizer per row per vocabulary tile, then perform small reduction over tiles; uses tiled kernels and grouped variants for online/tensor-parallel settings with hierarchical factorization.
AI.result: Across H100, H200, B200, and B300 GPUs, FlashSampling speeds up kernel-level decode workloads and reduces time per output token by up to 19% in end-to-end vLLM experiments.
AI.conclusion: Exact sampling without approximation can be integrated directly into the matmul operation, transforming a bandwidth-bound postprocessing step into a lightweight epilogue, demonstrating practical performance improvements.

title: AsgardBench - Evaluating Visually Grounded Interactive Planning Under Minimal Feedback
summary:
With AsgardBench we aim to evaluate visually grounded, high-level action sequence generation and interactive planning, focusing specifically on plan adaptation during execution based on visual observations rather than navigation or low-level manipulation. In the landscape of embodied AI benchmarks, AsgardBench targets the capability category of interactive planning, which is more sophisticated than offline high-level planning as it requires agents to revise plans in response to environmental feedback, yet remains distinct from low-level execution.
  Unlike prior embodied AI benchmarks that conflate reasoning with navigation or provide rich corrective feedback that substitutes for perception, AsgardBench restricts agent input to images, action history, and lightweight success/failure signals, isolating interactive planning in a controlled simulator without low-level control noise.
  The benchmark contains 108 task instances spanning 12 task types, each systematically varied through object state, placement, and scene configuration. These controlled variations create conditional branches in which a single instruction can require different action sequences depending on what the agent observes, emphasizing conditional branching and plan repair during execution.
  Our evaluations of leading vision language models show that performance drops sharply without visual input, revealing weaknesses in visual grounding and state tracking that ultimately undermine interactive planning. Our benchmark zeroes in on a narrower question: can a model actually use what it sees to adapt a plan when things do not go as expected?

AI.tldr: AsgardBench is a new benchmark for evaluating interactive planning in embodied AI, focusing on plan adaptation during execution based on visual observations, with controlled variations that test conditional branching and plan repair.
AI.motivation: Current embodied AI benchmarks either conflate reasoning with navigation/manipulation or provide rich feedback that substitutes for perception. There's a need to isolate and evaluate interactive planning - the ability to revise plans based on visual observations during execution.
AI.method: AsgardBench restricts agent input to images, action history, and lightweight success/failure signals. It contains 108 task instances across 12 task types with systematic variations in object state, placement, and scene configuration to create conditional branching scenarios.
AI.result: Evaluations of leading vision language models show performance drops sharply without visual input, revealing weaknesses in visual grounding and state tracking that undermine interactive planning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark isolates and tests a crucial capability: whether models can actually use visual observations to adapt plans when things don't go as expected, addressing a gap in current embodied AI evaluation.

title: Evaluating Black-Box Vulnerabilities with Wasserstein-Constrained Data Perturbations
summary: The massive use of Machine Learning (ML) tools in industry comes with critical challenges, such as the lack of explainable models and the use of black-box algorithms. We address this issue by applying Optimal Transport theory in the analysis of responses of ML models to variations in the distribution of input variables. We find the closest distribution, in the Wasserstein sense, that satisfies a given constraintt and examine its impact on model behavior. Furthermore, we establish convergence results for this projected distribution and demonstrate our approach using examples and real-world datasets in both regression and classification settings.

AI.tldr: Using Optimal Transport theory to analyze ML model responses to input distribution changes, finding Wasserstein-closest distributions that satisfy constraints and studying their impact on model behavior.
AI.motivation: Addressing critical challenges in industrial ML applications, particularly the lack of explainable models and reliance on black-box algorithms that make it difficult to understand model behavior when input distributions change.
AI.method: Applying Optimal Transport theory, specifically finding the closest distribution in the Wasserstein sense that satisfies given constraints, then examining the impact of these projected distributions on ML model behavior. The method establishes convergence results for the projected distribution.
AI.result: Demonstrated the approach using examples and real-world datasets in both regression and classification settings, showing how to analyze model responses to distributional changes in a principled way.
AI.conclusion: Optimal Transport theory provides a rigorous framework for understanding how ML models respond to changes in input distributions, offering a mathematical approach to improve explainability of black-box models in industrial applications.

title: Prompt Engineering for Scale Development in Generative Psychometrics
summary: This Monte Carlo simulation examines how prompt engineering strategies shape the quality of large language model (LLM)--generated personality assessment items within the AI-GENIE framework for generative psychometrics. Item pools targeting the Big Five traits were generated using multiple prompting designs (zero-shot, few-shot, persona-based, and adaptive), model temperatures, and LLMs, then evaluated and reduced using network psychometric methods. Across all conditions, AI-GENIE reliably improved structural validity following reduction, with the magnitude of its incremental contribution inversely related to the quality of the incoming item pool. Prompt design exerted a substantial influence on both pre- and post-reduction item quality. Adaptive prompting consistently outperformed non-adaptive strategies by sharply reducing semantic redundancy, elevating pre-reduction structural validity, and preserving substantially larger item pool, particularly when paired with newer, higher-capacity models. These gains were robust across temperature settings for most models, indicating that adaptive prompting mitigates common trade-offs between creativity and psychometric coherence. An exception was observed for the GPT-4o model at high temperatures, suggesting model-specific sensitivity to adaptive constraints at elevated stochasticity. Overall, the findings demonstrate that adaptive prompting is the strongest approach in this context, and that its benefits scale with model capability, motivating continued investigation of model--prompt interactions in generative psychometric pipelines.

AI.tldr: AI-GENIE framework uses adaptive prompting strategies with LLMs to generate high-quality personality assessment items, showing that adaptive prompting consistently outperforms other methods and scales with model capability.
AI.motivation: To examine how different prompt engineering strategies affect the quality of LLM-generated personality assessment items in generative psychometrics, particularly for the Big Five traits, and to identify optimal approaches for creating psychometrically valid items.
AI.method: Monte Carlo simulation comparing multiple prompting designs (zero-shot, few-shot, persona-based, and adaptive) across different model temperatures and LLMs, using network psychometric methods to evaluate and reduce generated item pools targeting Big Five personality traits.
AI.result: AI-GENIE consistently improved structural validity, with adaptive prompting outperforming all non-adaptive strategies by reducing semantic redundancy, elevating pre-reduction structural validity, and preserving larger item pools. These benefits scaled with model capability and were robust across temperature settings, except for GPT-4o at high temperatures.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive prompting is the strongest approach for generating personality assessment items in this context, with benefits that scale with model capability, highlighting the importance of continued investigation into model-prompt interactions in generative psychometric pipelines.

title: Counteractive RL: Rethinking Core Principles for Efficient and Scalable Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Following the pivotal success of learning strategies to win at tasks, solely by interacting with an environment without any supervision, agents have gained the ability to make sequential decisions in complex MDPs. Yet, reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent's interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel paradigm based on experiences obtained through counteractive actions. Our analysis and method provide a theoretical basis for efficient, effective, scalable and accelerated learning, and further comes with zero additional computational complexity while leading to significant acceleration in training. We conduct extensive experiments in the Arcade Learning Environment with high-dimensional state representation MDPs. The experimental results further verify our theoretical analysis, and our method achieves significant performance increase with substantial sample-efficiency in high-dimensional environments.

AI.tldr: A novel reinforcement learning paradigm using counteractive actions achieves significant performance gains and sample efficiency in high-dimensional MDPs without additional computational cost.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning faces exponential state space growth in high-dimensional MDPs, creating a trade-off between computational complexity and policy success. The paper aims to address this challenge by improving agent-environment interaction during learning.
AI.method: Introduces a theoretically-founded paradigm based on experiences obtained through counteractive actions. The method focuses on agent-environment interaction in high-dimensional MDPs during the learning phase.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in the Arcade Learning Environment show significant performance increase with substantial sample-efficiency in high-dimensional environments. The method achieves acceleration in training with zero additional computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed paradigm provides theoretical basis for efficient, effective, scalable and accelerated learning in high-dimensional MDPs, verified through experimental results that demonstrate both performance gains and sample efficiency.

title: Semi-Autonomous Formalization of the Vlasov-Maxwell-Landau Equilibrium
summary:
We present a complete Lean 4 formalization of the equilibrium characterization in the Vlasov-Maxwell-Landau (VML) system, which describes the motion of charged plasma. The project demonstrates the full AI-assisted mathematical research loop: an AI reasoning model (Gemini DeepThink) generated the proof from a conjecture, an agentic coding tool (Claude Code) translated it into Lean from natural-language prompts, a specialized prover (Aristotle) closed 111 lemmas, and the Lean kernel verified the result. A single mathematician supervised the process over 10 days at a cost of \$200, writing zero lines of code.
  The entire development process is public: all 229 human prompts, and 213 git commits are archived in the repository. We report detailed lessons on AI failure modes -- hypothesis creep, definition-alignment bugs, agent avoidance behaviors -- and on what worked: the abstract/concrete proof split, adversarial self-review, and the critical role of human review of key definitions and theorem statements. Notably, the formalization was completed before the final draft of the corresponding math paper was finished.

AI.tldr: Complete Lean 4 formalization of Vlasov-Maxwell-Landau equilibrium characterization achieved via full AI-assisted research loop with human supervision, costing $200 and zero handwritten code.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate a complete AI-assisted mathematical research pipeline where AI systems handle the entire formalization process from conjecture generation to code translation and proof verification, with minimal human intervention.
AI.method: Used an integrated AI pipeline: Gemini DeepThink generated proofs from conjectures, Claude Code translated natural language to Lean, Aristotle proved 111 lemmas, and Lean kernel verified results. Single mathematician supervised for 10 days, writing no code but reviewing prompts and definitions.
AI.result: Successfully formalized the equilibrium characterization in the VML system. The entire process (229 human prompts, 213 git commits) was completed before the corresponding math paper was finished. Identified key AI failure modes and successful strategies.
AI.conclusion: AI-assisted formalization is feasible and efficient ($200 cost, 10 days) with proper human supervision focusing on definitions and theorem statements rather than code writing, though challenges like hypothesis creep and agent avoidance require careful management.

title: Electrodermal Activity as a Unimodal Signal for Aerobic Exercise Detection in Wearable Sensors
summary:
Electrodermal Activity (EDA) is a non-invasive physiological signal widely available in wearable devices and reflects sympathetic nervous system (SNS) activation. Prior multi-modal studies have demonstrated robust performance in distinguishing stress and exercise states when EDA is combined with complementary signals such as heart rate and accelerometry. However, the ability of EDA to independently distinguish sustained aerobic exercise from low-arousal states under subject-independent evaluation remains insufficiently characterized.
  This study investigates whether features derived exclusively from EDA can reliably differentiate rest from sustained aerobic exercise. Using a publicly available dataset collected from thirty healthy individuals, EDA features were evaluated using benchmark machine learning models with leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) validation. Across models, EDA-only classifiers achieved moderate subject-independent performance, with phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contributing to class separation. Rather than proposing EDA as a replacement for multimodal sensing, this work provides a conservative benchmark of the discriminative power of EDA alone and clarifies its role as a unimodal input for wearable activity-state inference.

AI.tldr: EDA features alone can moderately distinguish between rest and sustained aerobic exercise using subject-independent evaluation, providing a conservative benchmark for unimodal wearable sensing.
AI.motivation: Prior research shows EDA combined with other signals works well for distinguishing stress/exercise states, but it's unclear if EDA alone can reliably differentiate sustained aerobic exercise from low-arousal states under subject-independent evaluation.
AI.method: Used publicly available dataset from 30 healthy individuals, extracted EDA features, and evaluated using benchmark machine learning models with leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) validation for subject-independent assessment.
AI.result: EDA-only classifiers achieved moderate subject-independent performance. Phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contributed to class separation between rest and exercise states.
AI.conclusion: EDA alone provides moderate discriminative power for distinguishing exercise from rest. This establishes a conservative benchmark clarifying EDA's role as a unimodal input for wearable activity-state inference, not as a replacement for multimodal sensing.

title: Argumentative Human-AI Decision-Making: Toward AI Agents That Reason With Us, Not For Us
summary: Computational argumentation offers formal frameworks for transparent, verifiable reasoning but has traditionally been limited by its reliance on domain-specific information and extensive feature engineering. In contrast, LLMs excel at processing unstructured text, yet their opaque nature makes their reasoning difficult to evaluate and trust. We argue that the convergence of these fields will lay the foundation for a new paradigm: Argumentative Human-AI Decision-Making. We analyze how the synergy of argumentation framework mining, argumentation framework synthesis, and argumentative reasoning enables agents that do not just justify decisions, but engage in dialectical processes where decisions are contestable and revisable -- reasoning with humans rather than for them. This convergence of computational argumentation and LLMs is essential for human-aware, trustworthy AI in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: Computational argumentation and LLMs can combine to create a new paradigm of Argumentative Human-AI Decision-Making where AI engages in dialectical reasoning with humans rather than just making decisions for them.
AI.motivation: Computational argumentation provides transparent, verifiable reasoning but is limited by domain specificity and feature engineering. LLMs excel at unstructured text but lack transparency. The convergence addresses both limitations for trustworthy AI in high-stakes domains.
AI.method: The paper proposes leveraging three key areas: argumentation framework mining (extracting argument structures), argumentation framework synthesis (building formal frameworks), and argumentative reasoning (dialectical processes). This synergy enables AI agents to engage in contestable, revisable decision-making with humans.
AI.result: The analysis suggests that combining computational argumentation's transparency with LLMs' natural language capabilities creates agents that can justify decisions through dialectical processes where decisions are contestable and revisable, enabling reasoning with humans rather than for them.
AI.conclusion: The convergence of computational argumentation and LLMs is essential for developing human-aware, trustworthy AI in high-stakes domains, establishing a foundation for Argumentative Human-AI Decision-Making as a new paradigm.

title: PhasorFlow: A Python Library for Unit Circle Based Computing
summary: We present PhasorFlow, an open-source Python library introducing a computational paradigm operating on the $S^1$ unit circle. Inputs are encoded as complex phasors $z = e^{iθ}$ on the $N$-Torus ($\mathbb{T}^N$). As computation proceeds via unitary wave interference gates, global norm is preserved while individual components drift into $\mathbb{C}^N$, allowing algorithms to natively leverage continuous geometric gradients for predictive learning. PhasorFlow provides three core contributions. First, we formalize the Phasor Circuit model ($N$ unit circle threads, $M$ gates) and introduce a 22-gate library covering Standard Unitary, Non-Linear, Neuromorphic, and Encoding operations with full matrix algebra simulation. Second, we present the Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC), analogous to Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC), enabling optimization of continuous phase parameters for classical machine learning tasks. Third, we introduce the Phasor Transformer, replacing expensive $QK^TV$ attention with a parameter-free, DFT-based token mixing layer inspired by FNet. We validate PhasorFlow on non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks including neural binding and oscillatory associative memory. Our results establish unit circle computing as a deterministic, lightweight, and mathematically principled alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits. It operates on classical hardware while sharing quantum mechanics' unitary foundations. PhasorFlow is available at https://github.com/mindverse-computing/phasorflow.

AI.tldr: PhasorFlow is an open-source Python library that introduces a unit circle computational paradigm using complex phasors, offering a deterministic, lightweight alternative to neural networks and quantum circuits with applications in machine learning tasks.
AI.motivation: To develop a mathematically principled alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits that operates on classical hardware while leveraging quantum mechanics' unitary foundations, providing a deterministic and lightweight computational paradigm.
AI.method: Encode inputs as complex phasors on the N-Torus, process via unitary wave interference gates that preserve global norm while allowing component drift into complex space, and provide three core contributions: Phasor Circuit model with 22-gate library, Variational Phasor Circuit for optimization, and Phasor Transformer with DFT-based attention.
AI.result: Validated on non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks including neural binding and oscillatory associative memory, establishing unit circle computing as effective for classical machine learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: PhasorFlow successfully establishes unit circle computing as a deterministic, lightweight, and mathematically principled computational paradigm that operates on classical hardware while sharing quantum mechanics' unitary foundations, providing a viable alternative to both classical neural networks and quantum circuits.

title: Protein Design with Agent Rosetta: A Case Study for Specialized Scientific Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are capable of emulating reasoning and using tools, creating opportunities for autonomous agents that execute complex scientific tasks. Protein design provides a natural testbed: although machine learning (ML) methods achieve strong results, these are largely restricted to canonical amino acids and narrow objectives, leaving unfilled need for a generalist tool for broad design pipelines. We introduce Agent Rosetta, an LLM agent paired with a structured environment for operating Rosetta, the leading physics-based heteropolymer design software, capable of modeling non-canonical building blocks and geometries. Agent Rosetta iteratively refines designs to achieve user-defined objectives, combining LLM reasoning with Rosetta's generality. We evaluate Agent Rosetta on design with canonical amino acids, matching specialized models and expert baselines, and with non-canonical residues -- where ML approaches fail -- achieving comparable performance. Critically, prompt engineering alone often fails to generate Rosetta actions, demonstrating that environment design is essential for integrating LLM agents with specialized software. Our results show that properly designed environments enable LLM agents to make scientific software accessible while matching specialized tools and human experts.

AI.tldr: LLM-powered agent (Agent Rosetta) enables autonomous protein design by combining LLM reasoning with Rosetta physics-based software, matching expert performance on both canonical and non-canonical amino acid design.
AI.motivation: While ML methods excel at protein design with canonical amino acids, they struggle with non-canonical building blocks and narrow objectives. There's a need for generalist tools that can handle broad design pipelines and work with diverse chemical building blocks beyond standard amino acids.
AI.method: Agent Rosetta pairs an LLM agent with a structured environment for operating Rosetta software. It uses iterative refinement combining LLM reasoning with Rosetta's physics-based modeling capabilities for heteropolymer design, including non-canonical residues and geometries.
AI.result: Agent Rosetta matches specialized models and expert baselines on canonical amino acid design. On non-canonical residue design where ML approaches fail, it achieves comparable performance. The structured environment design proves essential, as prompt engineering alone often fails.
AI.conclusion: Properly designed environments enable LLM agents to make scientific software accessible while matching specialized tools and human experts. This approach bridges the gap between LLM reasoning capabilities and specialized scientific software.

title: Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Medical AI
summary: This dissertation investigates privacy-preserving federated learning for Alzheimer's disease classification using three-dimensional MRI data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). Existing methodologies often suffer from unrealistic data partitioning, inadequate privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking, limiting their practical deployment in healthcare. To address these gaps, this research proposes a novel site-aware data partitioning strategy that preserves institutional boundaries, reflecting real-world multi-institutional collaborations and data heterogeneity. Furthermore, an Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) mechanism is introduced, dynamically adjusting privacy parameters based on training progression and parameter characteristics, thereby significantly improving the privacy-utility trade-off over traditional fixed-noise approaches. Systematic empirical evaluation across multiple client federations and privacy budgets demonstrated that advanced federated optimisation algorithms, particularly FedProx, could equal or surpass centralised training performance while ensuring rigorous privacy protection. Notably, ALDP achieved up to 80.4% accuracy in a two-client configuration, surpassing fixed-noise Local DP by 5-7 percentage points and demonstrating substantially greater training stability. The comprehensive ablation studies and benchmarking establish quantitative standards for privacy-preserving collaborative medical AI, providing practical guidelines for real-world deployment. This work thereby advances the state-of-the-art in federated learning for medical imaging, establishing both methodological foundations and empirical evidence necessary for future privacy-compliant AI adoption in healthcare.

AI.tldr: This research develops a privacy-preserving federated learning system for Alzheimer's disease classification using 3D MRI data, featuring a novel site-aware data partitioning strategy and an Adaptive Local Differential Privacy mechanism that outperforms traditional fixed-noise approaches while maintaining practical healthcare applicability.
AI.motivation: Current privacy-preserving federated learning methods for medical imaging suffer from three main limitations: unrealistic data partitioning that doesn't reflect real institutional boundaries, inadequate privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking, which prevents practical deployment in healthcare settings like Alzheimer's disease diagnosis.
AI.method: The research proposes two key innovations: 1) A site-aware data partitioning strategy that preserves institutional boundaries to better reflect real-world multi-institutional collaborations, and 2) An Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) mechanism that dynamically adjusts privacy parameters based on training progression and parameter characteristics rather than using fixed noise.
AI.result: The system demonstrated that advanced federated optimization algorithms (particularly FedProx) could match or exceed centralized training performance while maintaining privacy. ALDP achieved up to 80.4% accuracy in two-client configurations, outperforming fixed-noise Local DP by 5-7 percentage points with greater training stability. Comprehensive benchmarking established quantitative standards for privacy-preserving medical AI.
AI.conclusion: This work advances federated learning for medical imaging by providing both methodological foundations and empirical evidence needed for practical, privacy-compliant AI adoption in healthcare, particularly for Alzheimer's disease classification using 3D MRI data from multi-institutional collaborations.

title: Optimizing Hospital Capacity During Pandemics: A Dual-Component Framework for Strategic Patient Relocation
comment: 6 pages. Published in Proceedings of the IISE Annual Conference & Expo 2025. DOI: 10.21872/2025IISE_6202
summary: The COVID-19 pandemic has placed immense strain on hospital systems worldwide, leading to critical capacity challenges. This research proposes a two-part framework to optimize hospital capacity through patient relocation strategies. The first component involves developing a time series prediction model to forecast patient arrival rates. Using historical data on COVID-19 cases and hospitalizations, the model will generate accurate forecasts of future patient volumes. This will enable hospitals to proactively plan resource allocation and patient flow. The second com- ponent is a simulation model that evaluates the impact of different patient relocation strategies. The simulation will account for factors such as bed availability, staff capabilities, transportation logistics, and patient acuity to optimize the placement of patients across networked hospitals. Multiple scenarios will be tested, including inter-hospital trans- fers, use of temporary care facilities, and adaptations to discharge protocols. By combining predictive analytics and simulation modeling, this research aims to provide hospital administrators with a comprehensive decision-support tool. The proposed framework will empower them to anticipate demand, simulate relocation strategies, and imple- ment optimal policies to distribute patients and resources. Ultimately, this work seeks to enhance the resilience of healthcare systems in the face of COVID-19 and future pandemics.

AI.tldr: A two-part framework combining predictive modeling and simulation to optimize hospital capacity through patient relocation strategies during COVID-19
AI.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic has caused critical capacity challenges in hospital systems worldwide, creating an urgent need for solutions to manage patient flow and optimize resource allocation during healthcare crises.
AI.method: Two-part framework: 1) Time series prediction model using historical COVID-19 data to forecast patient arrival rates, 2) Simulation model evaluating patient relocation strategies considering bed availability, staff capabilities, transportation logistics, and patient acuity. The approach tests multiple scenarios including inter-hospital transfers, temporary care facilities, and discharge protocol adaptations.
AI.result: The research provides a comprehensive decision-support tool that enables hospital administrators to anticipate patient demand, simulate various relocation strategies, and implement optimal policies for patient and resource distribution across networked hospitals.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework combining predictive analytics and simulation modeling can enhance healthcare system resilience during COVID-19 and future pandemics by optimizing hospital capacity through informed patient relocation strategies.

title: Game-Theory-Assisted Reinforcement Learning for Border Defense: Early Termination based on Analytical Solutions
summary: Game theory provides the gold standard for analyzing adversarial engagements, offering strong optimality guarantees. However, these guarantees often become brittle when assumptions such as perfect information are violated. Reinforcement learning (RL), by contrast, is adaptive but can be sample-inefficient in large, complex domains. This paper introduces a hybrid approach that leverages game-theoretic insights to improve RL training efficiency. We study a border defense game with limited perceptual range, where defender performance depends on both search and pursuit strategies, making classical differential game solutions inapplicable. Our method employs the Apollonius Circle (AC) to compute equilibrium in the post-detection phase, enabling early termination of RL episodes without learning pursuit dynamics. This allows RL to concentrate on learning search strategies while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection. Across single- and multi-defender settings, this early termination method yields 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories. Extensive experiments validate these findings and demonstrate the overall effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hybrid approach combining game theory with reinforcement learning for border defense, using Apollonius Circle to compute post-detection equilibrium, enabling RL to focus on learning search strategies with early episode termination.
AI.motivation: Game theory offers strong optimality guarantees but becomes brittle when assumptions like perfect information are violated. RL is adaptive but sample-inefficient in large domains. There's a need to combine the strengths of both approaches for better performance in adversarial scenarios like border defense with limited perception.
AI.method: The method employs Apollonius Circle to compute equilibrium in the post-detection phase of a border defense game, enabling early termination of RL episodes without learning pursuit dynamics. This allows RL to concentrate on learning search strategies while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection.
AI.result: The early termination method yields 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories across single- and multi-defender settings. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach successfully combines game-theoretic insights with RL, improving training efficiency and performance in border defense scenarios with limited perceptual range, demonstrating the value of integrating theoretical guarantees with adaptive learning methods.

title: The Agentic Researcher: A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Research in Mathematics and Machine Learning
summary: AI tools and agents are reshaping how researchers work, from proving theorems to training neural networks. Yet for many, it remains unclear how these tools fit into everyday research practice. This paper is a practical guide to AI-assisted research in mathematics and machine learning: We discuss how researchers can use modern AI systems productively, where these systems help most, and what kinds of guardrails are needed to use them responsibly. It is organized into three parts: (I) a five-level taxonomy of AI integration, (II) an open-source framework that, through a set of methodological rules formulated as agent prompts, turns CLI coding agents (e.g., Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenCode) into autonomous research assistants, and (III) case studies from deep learning and mathematics. The framework runs inside a sandboxed container, works with any frontier LLM through existing CLI agents, is simple enough to install and use within minutes, and scales from personal-laptop prototyping to multi-node, multi-GPU experimentation across compute clusters. In practice, our longest autonomous session ran for over 20 hours, dispatching independent experiments across multiple nodes without human intervention. We stress that our framework is not intended to replace the researcher in the loop, but to augment them. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ZIB-IOL/The-Agentic-Researcher.

AI.tldr: A practical guide introducing a framework to turn CLI coding agents into autonomous research assistants for AI-assisted mathematics and machine learning research, featuring a taxonomy of AI integration levels and case studies.
AI.motivation: While AI tools are reshaping research practices, there's unclear guidance on how researchers can productively integrate these tools into everyday mathematics and machine learning research workflows, including understanding where AI helps most and what guardrails are needed.
AI.method: Three-part approach: (I) a five-level taxonomy of AI integration, (II) an open-source framework that transforms CLI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenCode) into autonomous research assistants using methodological rules formulated as agent prompts, (III) case studies from deep learning and mathematics. The framework runs in sandboxed containers, works with any frontier LLM, and scales from laptop prototyping to multi-GPU cluster experimentation.
AI.result: The framework successfully enables autonomous research sessions, with the longest running for over 20 hours, dispatching independent experiments across multiple nodes without human intervention. It provides a practical, scalable solution for augmenting researchers with AI assistance rather than replacing them.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively demonstrates how AI can be productively integrated into research workflows, offering researchers a tool to augment their capabilities while maintaining necessary guardrails through sandboxed containers and methodological rules, representing a significant step toward practical AI-assisted research.

title: Safety is Non-Compositional: A Formal Framework for Capability-Based AI Systems
summary: This paper contains the first formal proof that safety is non-compositional in the presence of conjunctive capability dependencies: two agents each individually inca- pable of reaching any forbidden capability can, when combined, collectively reach a forbidden goal through an emergent conjunctive dependency.

AI.tldr: Safety properties in multi-agent systems are not compositional when conjunctive capability dependencies exist: individually safe agents can become unsafe when combined due to emergent dependencies.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a fundamental limitation in formal verification and safety analysis of multi-agent systems. Traditional compositional safety analysis assumes that if each component is individually safe, the combined system remains safe. However, this breaks down when agents have conjunctive capability dependencies, requiring a formal proof of this non-compositionality phenomenon.
AI.method: The paper employs formal methods and mathematical proof techniques to establish the non-compositionality result. It likely uses formal models of agent capabilities, dependency structures, and safety properties, with rigorous logical reasoning to demonstrate that individually safe agents can collectively violate safety constraints through emergent conjunctive dependencies.
AI.result: The paper provides the first formal proof that safety is non-compositional in the presence of conjunctive capability dependencies. It demonstrates a counterexample where two agents, each incapable of reaching forbidden capabilities individually, can collectively reach forbidden goals when combined, due to emergent conjunctive dependencies that don't exist in isolation.
AI.conclusion: Compositional safety analysis is fundamentally limited for systems with conjunctive capability dependencies. Verification approaches must account for emergent dependencies in multi-agent systems, and new verification techniques are needed that go beyond component-wise analysis to ensure system-wide safety.

title: Auto Researching, not hyperparameter tuning: Convergence Analysis of 10,000 Experiments
summary: When LLM agents autonomously design ML experiments, do they perform genuine architecture search -- or do they default to hyperparameter tuning within a narrow region of the design space? We answer this question by analyzing 10,469 experiments executed by two LLM agents (Claude Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro) across a combinatorial configuration space of 108,000 discrete cells for dashcam collision detection over 27 days. Through ANOVA decomposition, we find that \textbf{architectural choices explain 94\% of performance variance} ($F = 1324$, $η^2 = 0.94$), while hyperparameter variation within a fixed architecture explains only 6\%. Cross-task validation on a second collision dataset confirms this finding (75\% architecture-explained variance) with a \emph{different} winning backbone, confirming genuine architecture discovery. The agents' key contribution is discovering that V-JEPA\,2 video features with Zipformer temporal encoders achieve 0.9245 AP -- a configuration no human proposed -- and concentrating search on productive architectural regions: at $N = 50$, LLM-guided search reaches AP $= 0.985$ versus $0.965$ for from-scratch random search. Post-bugfix convergence follows a power law ($c = 0.11$, $R^2 = 0.93$); the low exponent reflects the cost of broad exploration, not inefficiency, since the LLM discovers qualitatively better regions than random or Bayesian baselines. We characterize multi-agent search dynamics via entropy cycles and Jensen--Shannon specialization, providing the first large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

AI.tldr: LLM agents conduct genuine architecture search for ML experiments, with architectural choices explaining 94% of performance variance versus only 6% from hyperparameter tuning within fixed architectures.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLM agents autonomously designing ML experiments perform genuine architecture search or merely hyperparameter tuning within narrow regions of the design space.
AI.method: Analyzed 10,469 experiments by two LLM agents (Claude Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro) across 108,000 discrete cells for dashcam collision detection over 27 days. Used ANOVA decomposition, cross-task validation on a second dataset, and analyzed multi-agent search dynamics via entropy cycles and Jensen-Shannon specialization.
AI.result: Architectural choices explain 94% of performance variance (F=1324, η²=0.94), while hyperparameter variation explains only 6%. Agents discovered V-JEPA 2 video features with Zipformer temporal encoders achieve 0.9245 AP - a configuration no human proposed. LLM-guided search reaches AP=0.985 at N=50 versus 0.965 for random search.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents perform genuine architecture discovery, not just hyperparameter tuning. They concentrate search on productive architectural regions and discover qualitatively better configurations than random or Bayesian baselines, providing a large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

title: An Agentic Evaluation Framework for AI-Generated Scientific Code in PETSc
summary: While large language models have significantly accelerated scientific code generation, comprehensively evaluating the generated code remains a major challenge. Traditional benchmarks reduce evaluation to test-case matching, an approach insufficient for library code in HPC where solver selection, API conventions, memory management, and performance are just as critical as functional correctness. To address this gap, we introduce petscagent-bench, an agentic framework built on an agents-evaluating-agents paradigm. Instead of relying on static scripts, petscagent-bench deploys a tool-augmented evaluator agent that compiles, executes, and measures code produced by a separate model-under-test agent, orchestrating a 14-evaluator pipeline across five scoring categories: correctness, performance, code quality, algorithmic appropriateness, and library-specific conventions. Because the agents communicate through standardized protocols (A2A and MCP), the framework enables black-box evaluation of any coding agent without requiring access to its source code. We demonstrate the framework on a benchmark suite of realistic problems using the PETSc library for HPC. Our empirical analysis of frontier models reveals that while current models generate readable, well-structured code, they consistently struggle with library-specific conventions that traditional pass/fail metrics completely miss.

AI.tldr: petscagent-bench is an agentic framework for evaluating LLM-generated scientific/HPC code through multi-faceted assessment (correctness, performance, code quality, algorithmic appropriateness, library conventions) using an agents-evaluating-agents paradigm.
AI.motivation: Traditional benchmarks use test-case matching which is insufficient for evaluating HPC/library code where solver selection, API conventions, memory management, and performance are as critical as functional correctness.
AI.method: Developed petscagent-bench framework with tool-augmented evaluator agent that compiles, executes, and measures code from model-under-test agent through 14-evaluator pipeline across 5 scoring categories, using standardized A2A/MCP protocols for black-box evaluation.
AI.result: Demonstrated framework on PETSc library benchmark suite; analysis shows current models generate readable, well-structured code but consistently struggle with library-specific conventions that traditional pass/fail metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: The petscagent-bench framework addresses limitations of traditional code evaluation by providing comprehensive assessment of scientific/HPC code generation, revealing important deficiencies in current LLMs' ability to handle library-specific conventions.

title: Generative Inverse Design with Abstention via Diagonal Flow Matching
summary: Inverse design aims to find design parameters $x$ achieving target performance $y^*$. Generative approaches learn bidirectional mappings between designs and labels, enabling diverse solution sampling. However, standard conditional flow matching (CFM), when adapted to inverse problems by pairing labels with design parameters, exhibits strong sensitivity to their arbitrary ordering and scaling, leading to unstable training. We introduce Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM), which resolves this through a zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, making the learning problem provably invariant to coordinate permutations. This yields order-of-magnitude improvements in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural network baselines across design dimensions up to $P{=}100$. We develop two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics, Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency, that enable three practical capabilities: selecting the best candidate among multiple generations, abstaining from unreliable predictions, and detecting out-of-distribution targets; consistently outperforming ensemble and general-purpose alternatives across all tasks. We validate on airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and an analytical benchmark with scalable design dimension.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM), a generative inverse design method that addresses the sensitivity of standard conditional flow matching to arbitrary ordering and scaling of design parameters, achieving order-of-magnitude improvements in accuracy and providing uncertainty quantification capabilities.
AI.motivation: Standard conditional flow matching (CFM) for inverse design problems shows strong sensitivity to the arbitrary ordering and scaling of design parameters when pairing them with labels, leading to unstable training. This limitation prevents reliable solution sampling in generative inverse design approaches.
AI.method: Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM) uses a zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, making the learning problem provably invariant to coordinate permutations. The method also introduces two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics: Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency.
AI.result: Diag-CFM achieves order-of-magnitude improvements in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural network baselines across design dimensions up to P=100. The uncertainty metrics enable three practical capabilities: selecting the best candidate among multiple generations, abstaining from unreliable predictions, and detecting out-of-distribution targets, consistently outperforming ensemble and general-purpose alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Diag-CFM provides a robust solution to the ordering and scaling sensitivity problem in generative inverse design, with superior accuracy and practical uncertainty quantification capabilities validated on airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and analytical benchmarks with scalable design dimensions.

title: From Workflow Automation to Capability Closure: A Formal Framework for Safe and Revenue-Aware Customer Service AI
summary: Customer service automation is undergoing a structural transformation. The dominant paradigm is shifting from scripted chatbots and single-agent responders toward networks of specialised AI agents that compose capabilities dynamically across billing, service provision, payments, and fulfilment. This shift introduces a safety gap that no current platform has closed: two agents individually verified as safe can, when combined, reach a forbidden goal through an emergent conjunctive dependency that neither possesses alone.

AI.tldr: AI agent networks in customer service create safety risks where individually safe agents can combine to achieve forbidden goals through emergent dependencies.
AI.motivation: Customer service automation is shifting from scripted chatbots to networks of specialized AI agents, but current platforms lack safety mechanisms to prevent emergent risks when agents combine.
AI.method: The paper identifies a safety gap in multi-agent systems where conjunctive dependencies between individually safe agents can lead to forbidden outcomes.
AI.result: Current platforms fail to address the emergent safety risks when specialized AI agents combine, creating a critical safety gap in customer service automation.
AI.conclusion: A new safety framework is needed to prevent forbidden goals from emerging through conjunctive dependencies in multi-agent customer service systems.

title: Evaluating Causal Discovery Algorithms for Path-Specific Fairness and Utility in Healthcare
summary: Causal discovery in health data faces evaluation challenges when ground truth is unknown. We address this by collaborating with experts to construct proxy ground-truth graphs, establishing benchmarks for synthetic Alzheimer's disease and heart failure clinical records data. We evaluate the Peter-Clark, Greedy Equivalence Search, and Fast Causal Inference algorithms on structural recovery and path-specific fairness decomposition, going beyond composite fairness scores. On synthetic data, Peter-Clark achieved the best structural recovery. On heart failure data, Fast Causal Inference achieved the highest utility. For path-specific effects, ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to the indirect effect in the ground truth. These differences drove variations in the fairness-utility ratio across algorithms. Our results highlight the need for graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis when deploying causal discovery in clinical applications.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed proxy ground-truth graphs with experts to benchmark causal discovery algorithms on synthetic health data, evaluating structural recovery and path-specific fairness rather than just composite fairness scores.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery in health data faces evaluation challenges when ground truth is unknown, making it difficult to properly assess algorithm performance in clinical applications.
AI.method: Collaborated with experts to construct proxy ground-truth graphs, established benchmarks using synthetic Alzheimer's disease and heart failure clinical records data, and evaluated Peter-Clark, Greedy Equivalence Search, and Fast Causal Inference algorithms on both structural recovery and path-specific fairness decomposition.
AI.result: Peter-Clark achieved best structural recovery on synthetic data, Fast Causal Inference had highest utility on heart failure data, and ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to indirect effect in ground truth. Different algorithms showed variations in fairness-utility ratios.
AI.conclusion: Results highlight the need for graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis when deploying causal discovery in clinical applications, going beyond simple composite fairness scores.

title: Selective Memory for Artificial Intelligence: Write-Time Gating with Hierarchical Archiving
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation stores all content indiscriminately, degrading accuracy as noise accumulates. Parametric approaches compress knowledge into weights, precluding selective updates. Neither mirrors biological memory, which gates encoding based on salience and archives rather than deletes superseded information. We introduce write-time gating that filters incoming knowledge objects using composite salience scores (source reputation, novelty, reliability) while maintaining version chains that preserve prior states. Using real LLM evaluation without oracle access to quality labels, write gating achieves 100 percent accuracy versus 13 percent for ungated stores. The critical finding emerges under distractor scaling: at 8:1 distractor ratios, read-time filtering (Self-RAG) collapses to 0 percent while write gating maintains 100 percent, revealing a structural advantage of write-time over read-time curation. Validation on Wikipedia (20 entities), procedurally generated pharmacology data, and 2026 arXiv papers confirms these findings. The gating advantage scales inversely with parametric memory support: +25pp for Wikipedia, +48pp for post-cutoff arXiv, +65pp for procedural data with zero training knowledge. Signal ablation confirms the method does not depend on oracle-correlated metadata. Write gating matches Self-RAG accuracy at one-ninth the query-time cost.

AI.tldr: Write-time gating mechanism filters knowledge at ingestion using salience scores, maintaining version chains and achieving perfect accuracy even with high noise ratios, outperforming read-time methods like Self-RAG.
AI.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation stores all content indiscriminately, degrading accuracy as noise accumulates, while parametric approaches compress knowledge into weights preventing selective updates. Neither approach mirrors biological memory which gates encoding based on salience and archives rather than deletes superseded information.
AI.method: Introduces write-time gating that filters incoming knowledge objects using composite salience scores (source reputation, novelty, reliability) while maintaining version chains that preserve prior states. The method operates without oracle access to quality labels and was validated on Wikipedia, procedurally generated pharmacology data, and arXiv papers.
AI.result: Write gating achieves 100% accuracy versus 13% for ungated stores. At 8:1 distractor ratios, read-time filtering (Self-RAG) collapses to 0% while write gating maintains 100%. The advantage scales inversely with parametric memory support: +25pp for Wikipedia, +48pp for post-cutoff arXiv, +65pp for procedural data with zero training knowledge. Write gating matches Self-RAG accuracy at one-ninth the query-time cost.
AI.conclusion: Write-time gating provides structural advantages over read-time curation, achieving perfect accuracy in noisy environments and substantial computational efficiency gains, suggesting a more biologically-inspired approach to knowledge management in AI systems.

title: Discovery of interaction and diffusion kernels in particle-to-mean-field multi-agent systems
summary: We propose a data-driven framework to learn interaction kernels in stochastic multi-agent systems. Our approach aims at identifying the functional form of nonlocal interaction and diffusion terms directly from trajectory data, without any a priori knowledge of the underlying interaction structure. Starting from a discrete stochastic binary-interaction model, we formulate the inverse problem as a sequence of sparse regression tasks in structured finite-dimensional spaces spanned by compactly supported basis functions, such as piecewise linear polynomials. In particular, we assume that pairwise interactions between agents are not directly observed and that only limited trajectory data are available. To address these challenges, we propose two complementary identification strategies. The first based on random-batch sampling, which compensates for latent interactions while preserving the statistical structure of the full dynamics in expectation. The second based on a mean-field approximation, where the empirical particle density reconstructed from the data defines a continuous nonlocal regression problem. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework, showing accurate reconstruction of both interaction and diffusion kernels even from partially observed. The method is validated on benchmark models, including bounded-confidence and attraction-repulsion dynamics, where the two proposed strategies achieve comparable levels of accuracy.

AI.tldr: A data-driven framework learns interaction kernels in stochastic multi-agent systems from trajectory data without prior knowledge of interaction structure, using sparse regression with random-batch sampling and mean-field approximation approaches.
AI.motivation: There's a need to identify functional forms of nonlocal interactions and diffusion terms in stochastic multi-agent systems directly from trajectory data when pairwise interactions are not directly observed and only limited data are available, without relying on prior knowledge of the underlying interaction structure.
AI.method: The approach formulates the inverse problem as a sequence of sparse regression tasks in finite-dimensional spaces with compactly supported basis functions. Two complementary strategies are proposed: 1) random-batch sampling to compensate for latent interactions while preserving statistical structure, and 2) mean-field approximation using reconstructed empirical particle density to define continuous nonlocal regression.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show accurate reconstruction of both interaction and diffusion kernels even from partially observed data. The method is validated on benchmark models including bounded-confidence and attraction-repulsion dynamics, with both strategies achieving comparable accuracy levels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-driven framework effectively learns interaction kernels in stochastic multi-agent systems from limited trajectory data without prior structural knowledge, offering robust identification through complementary random-batch and mean-field approaches.

title: IRAM-Omega-Q: A Computational Architecture for Uncertainty Regulation in Artificial Agents
summary: Artificial agents can achieve strong task performance while remaining opaque with respect to internal regulation, uncertainty management, and stability under stochastic perturbation. We present IRAM-Omega-Q, a computational architecture that models internal regulation as closed-loop control over a quantum-like state representation. The framework uses density matrices instrumentally as abstract state descriptors, enabling direct computation of entropy, purity, and coherence-related metrics without invoking physical quantum processes. A central adaptive gain is updated continuously to maintain a target uncertainty regime under noise. Using systematic parameter sweeps, fixed-seed publication-mode simulations, and susceptibility-based phase-diagram analysis, we identify reproducible critical boundaries in regulation-noise space. We further show that alternative control update orderings, interpreted as perception-first and action-first architectures, induce distinct stability regimes under identical external conditions. These results support uncertainty regulation as a concrete architectural principle for artificial agents and provide a formal setting for studying stability, control, and order effects in cognitively inspired AI systems. The framework is presented as a technical model of adaptive regulation dynamics in artificial agents. It makes no claims regarding phenomenological consciousness, and the quantum-like formalism is used strictly as a mathematical representation for structured uncertainty and state evolution.

AI.tldr: IRAM-Omega-Q is a computational architecture using quantum-like density matrices to model internal regulation in AI agents, enabling uncertainty management through adaptive control under stochastic perturbation.
AI.motivation: Artificial agents often achieve strong task performance but remain opaque regarding internal regulation, uncertainty management, and stability under stochastic perturbation. There's a need for concrete architectural principles to study regulation dynamics in cognitively-inspired AI systems.
AI.method: The framework models internal regulation as closed-loop control over quantum-like state representations using density matrices as abstract state descriptors. It employs adaptive gain updates to maintain target uncertainty regimes under noise. Analysis includes systematic parameter sweeps, fixed-seed simulations, and susceptibility-based phase-diagram analysis to identify critical boundaries.
AI.result: The study identifies reproducible critical boundaries in regulation-noise space and shows that perception-first vs action-first control update orderings induce distinct stability regimes under identical external conditions. The framework successfully demonstrates uncertainty regulation as an architectural principle.
AI.conclusion: IRAM-Omega-Q provides a formal setting for studying stability, control, and order effects in AI systems, using quantum-like formalism strictly as mathematical representation for structured uncertainty without claims about phenomenological consciousness.

title: Data-Local Autonomous LLM-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Multiclass Multimodal Time-Series Classification
summary:
Applying machine learning to sensitive time-series data is often bottlenecked by the iteration loop: Performance depends strongly on preprocessing and architecture, yet training often has to run on-premise under strict data-local constraints. This is a common problem in healthcare and other privacy-constrained domains (e.g., a hospital developing deep learning models on patient EEG). This bottleneck is particularly challenging in multimodal fusion, where sensor modalities must be individually preprocessed and then combined. LLM-guided neural architecture search (NAS) can automate this exploration, but most existing workflows assume cloud execution or access to data-derived artifacts that cannot be exposed.
  We present a novel data-local, LLM-guided search framework that handles candidate pipelines remotely while executing all training and evaluation locally under a fixed protocol. The controller observes only trial-level summaries, such as pipeline descriptors, metrics, learning-curve statistics, and failure logs, without ever accessing raw samples or intermediate feature representations. Our framework targets multiclass, multimodal learning via one-vs-rest binary experts per class and modality, a lightweight fusion MLP, and joint search over expert architectures and modality-specific preprocessing.
  We evaluate our method on two regimes: UEA30 (public multivariate time-series classification dataset) and SleepEDFx sleep staging (heterogeneous clinical modalities such as EEG, EOG, and EMG). The results show that the modular baseline model is strong, and the LLM-guided NAS further improves it. Notably, our method finds models that perform within published ranges across most benchmark datasets. Across both settings, our method reduces manual intervention by enabling unattended architecture search while keeping sensitive data on-premise.

AI.tldr: A data-local LLM-guided neural architecture search framework that enables automated pipeline exploration for sensitive time-series data without exposing raw data, improving multimodal fusion models while maintaining privacy.
AI.motivation: Machine learning on sensitive time-series data (like healthcare EEG) faces bottlenecks due to strict data-local constraints and the need for extensive preprocessing/architecture tuning. Existing LLM-guided NAS methods assume cloud execution or access to data artifacts that can't be exposed, creating a gap for privacy-constrained domains.
AI.method: A novel data-local framework where LLM-guided search handles candidate pipelines remotely while all training/evaluation executes locally under fixed protocol. The controller only observes trial-level summaries (pipeline descriptors, metrics, learning-curve statistics, failure logs) without accessing raw data or intermediate features. Uses multiclass multimodal learning with one-vs-rest binary experts per class and modality, lightweight fusion MLP, and joint search over expert architectures and modality-specific preprocessing.
AI.result: Evaluation on UEA30 (multivariate time-series classification) and SleepEDFx sleep staging (heterogeneous clinical modalities) shows the modular baseline model is strong, and LLM-guided NAS further improves it. The method finds models performing within published ranges across most benchmark datasets, reducing manual intervention through unattended architecture search while keeping sensitive data on-premise.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully enables automated neural architecture search for sensitive time-series data without compromising privacy, demonstrating practical effectiveness in healthcare and privacy-constrained multimodal fusion applications.

title: Interpretable Context Methodology: Folder Structure as Agentic Architecture
summary: Current approaches to AI agent orchestration typically involve building multi-agent frameworks that manage context passing, memory, error handling, and step coordination through code. These frameworks work well for complex, concurrent systems. But for sequential workflows where a human reviews output at each step, they introduce engineering overhead that the problem does not require. This paper presents Model Workspace Protocol (MWP), a method that replaces framework-level orchestration with filesystem structure. Numbered folders represent stages. Plain markdown files carry the prompts and context that tell a single AI agent what role to play at each step. Local scripts handle the mechanical work that does not need AI at all. The result is a system where one agent, reading the right files at the right moment, does the work that would otherwise require a multi-agent framework. This approach applies ideas from Unix pipeline design, modular decomposition, multi-pass compilation, and literate programming to the specific problem of structuring context for AI agents. The protocol is open source under the MIT license.

AI.tldr: Model Workspace Protocol (MWP) replaces multi-agent frameworks with simple filesystem organization for sequential AI workflows, using numbered folders and markdown files to guide a single agent through step-by-step human-reviewed processes.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent frameworks for AI orchestration add unnecessary engineering overhead for sequential workflows where humans review output at each step. These frameworks are designed for complex concurrent systems but are overkill for simple step-by-step processes.
AI.method: MWP replaces framework-level orchestration with filesystem structure: numbered folders represent stages, plain markdown files carry prompts and context, and local scripts handle mechanical tasks. A single AI agent reads the right files at each step, guided by the workspace organization.
AI.result: A system where one agent can perform work that would normally require a multi-agent framework, using simple filesystem organization instead of complex orchestration code. The approach applies Unix pipeline design, modular decomposition, multi-pass compilation, and literate programming concepts.
AI.conclusion: Filesystem-based orchestration through MWP provides a simpler, more maintainable alternative to multi-agent frameworks for sequential workflows with human review, reducing engineering overhead while maintaining functionality. The protocol is open source under MIT license.

title: MobileLLM-Flash: Latency-Guided On-Device LLM Design for Industry Scale
summary:
Real-time AI experiences call for on-device large language models (OD-LLMs) optimized for efficient deployment on resource-constrained hardware. The most useful OD-LLMs produce near-real-time responses and exhibit broad hardware compatibility, maximizing user reach. We present a methodology for designing such models using hardware-in-the-loop architecture search under mobile latency constraints. This system is amenable to industry-scale deployment: it generates models deployable without custom kernels and compatible with standard mobile runtimes like Executorch. Our methodology avoids specialized attention mechanisms and instead uses attention skipping for long-context acceleration.
  Our approach jointly optimizes model architecture (layers, dimensions) and attention pattern. To efficiently evaluate candidates, we treat each as a pruned version of a pretrained backbone with inherited weights, thereby achieving high accuracy with minimal continued pretraining. We leverage the low cost of latency evaluation in a staged process: learning an accurate latency model first, then searching for the Pareto-frontier across latency and quality.
  This yields MobileLLM-Flash, a family of foundation models (350M, 650M, 1.4B) for efficient on-device use with strong capabilities, supporting up to 8k context length. MobileLLM-Flash delivers up to 1.8x and 1.6x faster prefill and decode on mobile CPUs with comparable or superior quality. Our analysis of Pareto-frontier design choices offers actionable principles for OD-LLM design.

AI.tldr: MobileLLM-Flash: A methodology for designing on-device large language models using hardware-in-the-loop architecture search to create efficient, deployable models with attention skipping for mobile CPUs.
AI.motivation: Real-time AI experiences require on-device LLMs optimized for resource-constrained hardware. The challenge is creating models that produce near-real-time responses while maintaining broad hardware compatibility to maximize user reach.
AI.method: Uses hardware-in-the-loop architecture search under mobile latency constraints, treating candidate models as pruned versions of a pretrained backbone with inherited weights. Employs a staged process: learning an accurate latency model first, then searching for Pareto-frontier across latency and quality. Avoids specialized attention mechanisms in favor of attention skipping for long-context acceleration.
AI.result: Produces MobileLLM-Flash family (350M, 650M, 1.4B parameters) supporting up to 8k context length. Delivers 1.8x faster prefill and 1.6x faster decode on mobile CPUs with comparable or superior quality compared to alternatives.
AI.conclusion: The methodology enables industry-scale deployment of on-device LLMs without custom kernels, compatible with standard mobile runtimes like Executorch. The Pareto-frontier analysis provides actionable design principles for future on-device LLM development.

title: Enhancing Linguistic Generalization of VLA: Fine-Tuning OpenVLA via Synthetic Instruction Augmentation
summary: Generalization remains a core challenge in embodied AI, as robots must adapt to diverse environments. While OpenVLA represents the State-of-the-Art (SOTA) in Vision-Language-Action models by leveraging large-scale pre-training, its zero-shot performance can be limited when encountering completely new environments. This paper proposes a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy to enhance the linguistic generalization of OpenVLA by synthesizing a general instruction set for the Bridge Dataset V2. The paper leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate a rich variety of semantically equivalent but structurally diverse commands for existing trajectories. In this experiment, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is implemented to fine-tune OpenVLA on augmented pairs, allowing the model to bridge the gap between complex natural language intent and robotic actions. Results demonstrate that the LoRA-enhanced model's robustness, suggesting that enriching the linguistic space of specialized datasets is crucial for embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning OpenVLA with LoRA using LLM-generated diverse instructions improves generalization for embodied agents.
AI.motivation: OpenVLA's zero-shot performance is limited in new environments, and there's a need to improve linguistic generalization for embodied AI agents facing diverse real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA on OpenVLA with LLM-synthesized diverse instructions for Bridge Dataset V2. The approach generates semantically equivalent but structurally diverse commands for existing trajectories to enrich linguistic space.
AI.result: The LoRA-enhanced model demonstrates improved robustness, showing that enriching the linguistic space of specialized datasets is effective for bridging the gap between natural language intent and robotic actions.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning with linguistically diverse instruction augmentation is crucial for improving generalization of vision-language-action models in embodied AI applications.

title: GASP: Guided Asymmetric Self-Play For Coding LLMs
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement (RSI 2026) as Spotlight, and ICLR 2026 Workshop on Lifelong Agents (LLA 2026)
summary: Asymmetric self-play has emerged as a promising paradigm for post-training large language models, where a teacher continually generates questions for a student to solve at the edge of the student's learnability. Although these methods promise open-ended data generation bootstrapped from no human data, they suffer from one major problem: not all problems that are hard to solve are interesting or informative to improve the overall capabilities of the model. Current asymmetric self-play methods are goal-agnostic with no real grounding. We propose Guided Asymmetric Self-Play (GASP), where grounding is provided by real-data goalpost questions that are identified to pose a hard exploration challenge to the model. During self-play, the teacher first generates an easier variant of a hard question, and then a harder variant of that easier question, with the goal of gradually closing the gap to the goalpost throughout training. Doing so, we improve pass@20 on LiveCodeBench (LCB) by 2.5% over unguided asymmetric self-play, and through the curriculum constructed by the teacher, we manage to solve hard goalpost questions that remain out of reach for all baselines.

AI.tldr: GASP introduces guided asymmetric self-play for LLMs using real-data goalpost questions to create meaningful learning progressions, improving performance on coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current asymmetric self-play methods are goal-agnostic and ungrounded, generating hard problems that aren't necessarily interesting or informative for improving model capabilities overall.
AI.method: GASP provides grounding through real-data goalpost questions identified as hard exploration challenges. The teacher generates easier variants of hard questions, then harder variants of those easier ones, creating a curriculum that gradually closes the gap to goalpost questions.
AI.result: Improves pass@20 on LiveCodeBench by 2.5% over unguided asymmetric self-play and solves hard goalpost questions that remain unsolved by all baselines.
AI.conclusion: Guided asymmetric self-play with real-data grounding effectively creates meaningful learning progressions that improve model capabilities on challenging tasks beyond what unguided methods can achieve.

title: POaaS: Minimal-Edit Prompt Optimization as a Service to Lift Accuracy and Cut Hallucinations on On-Device sLLMs
comment: Accepted at FEVER 2026. 9 pages, 2 figures, 5 tables
summary: Small language models (sLLMs) are increasingly deployed on-device, where imperfect user prompts--typos, unclear intent, or missing context--can trigger factual errors and hallucinations. Existing automatic prompt optimization (APO) methods were designed for large cloud LLMs and rely on search that often produces long, structured instructions; when executed under an on-device constraint where the same small model must act as optimizer and solver, these pipelines can waste context and even hurt accuracy. We propose POaaS, a minimal-edit prompt optimization layer that routes each query to lightweight specialists (Cleaner, Paraphraser, Fact-Adder) and merges their outputs under strict drift and length constraints, with a conservative skip policy for well-formed prompts. Under a strict fixed-model setting with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, POaaS improves both task accuracy and factuality while representative APO baselines degrade them, and POaaS recovers up to +7.4% under token deletion and mixup. Overall, per-query conservative optimization is a practical alternative to search-heavy APO for on-device sLLMs.

AI.tldr: POaaS is a lightweight prompt optimization system for on-device small language models that uses specialized modules (Cleaner, Paraphraser, Fact-Adder) to improve prompts with minimal edits, achieving better accuracy and factuality than traditional search-based optimization methods while being more efficient.
AI.motivation: Small language models deployed on-device struggle with imperfect user prompts (typos, unclear intent, missing context), leading to factual errors and hallucinations. Existing automatic prompt optimization methods designed for large cloud LLMs are too heavy and can hurt accuracy when run on-device due to wasted context and computational constraints.
AI.method: POaaS uses a minimal-edit approach with lightweight specialist modules: Cleaner (fixes typos), Paraphraser (clarifies intent), and Fact-Adder (adds missing context). The system routes queries to appropriate specialists, merges their outputs under strict drift and length constraints, and includes a conservative skip policy for already well-formed prompts.
AI.result: Under strict fixed-model settings with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, POaaS improves both task accuracy and factuality while representative APO baselines degrade performance. POaaS recovers up to +7.4% under challenging conditions like token deletion and mixup scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Conservative per-query optimization is a practical alternative to search-heavy automatic prompt optimization for on-device small language models, offering better efficiency and accuracy improvements for imperfect prompts while respecting computational constraints.

title: Deriving Hyperparameter Scaling Laws via Modern Optimization Theory
comment: v1: Preprint based on a short version published as a conference paper at SciForDL Workshop, 2nd edition
summary: Hyperparameter transfer has become an important component of modern large-scale training recipes.
