The results suggest that while overhead exists, further work to reduce propagation overhead could yield additional performance improvements.

title: Trained Persistent Memory for Frozen Encoder--Decoder LLMs: Six Architectural Methods
summary: Frozen encoder--decoder language models are stateless: the latent representation is discarded after every forward pass, so no information persists across sessions. This paper presents a \textbf{proof-of-concept pilot study} showing that persistent memory in the \emph{continuous latent space} of a frozen LLM is feasible -- even under severe resource constraints (a single frozen Flan-T5-XL backbone, small trainable adapters, a single dataset). We implement six architectural methods spanning three injection points and four write mechanisms; unlike text-level memory systems, every write and read is a differentiable operation on dense vectors. After training only the adapter, the memory bank continues to accumulate at inference time without gradients, enabling \emph{conversational learning}. Under a forgetting-curve evaluation on LoCoMo at two capacity scales (1$\times$ and 10$\times$), the stateless baseline scores exactly zero; at 10$\times$ all six trained adapters produce positive memory-recall curves; at 1$\times$ three methods collapse, revealing capacity as a critical design parameter. Because the memory bank is a compact numerical array, it can be scaled to arbitrarily large capacity without altering the backbone. We argue that full end-to-end training with larger models, larger data, and orders-of-magnitude larger memory will yield substantially stronger results; this pilot study establishes the feasibility baseline and design-space taxonomy that such efforts require.

AI.tldr: Proof-of-concept study shows persistent differentiable memory in frozen LLM latent spaces is feasible, enabling conversational learning through memory banks without retraining the backbone model.
AI.motivation: Current frozen encoder-decoder language models are stateless - they discard latent representations after each forward pass, losing information across sessions. The paper aims to demonstrate that persistent memory in continuous latent spaces is possible despite resource constraints.
AI.method: Six architectural methods implemented spanning three injection points and four write mechanisms, using a single frozen Flan-T5-XL backbone with small trainable adapters. All operations (writes and reads) are differentiable operations on dense vectors. After training only the adapter, the memory bank accumulates at inference time without gradients.
AI.result: Under forgetting-curve evaluation on LoCoMo at two capacity scales (1× and 10×), stateless baseline scored zero. At 10× capacity, all six trained adapters produced positive memory-recall curves. At 1× capacity, three methods collapsed, showing capacity as critical design parameter. Memory banks can scale arbitrarily without altering backbone.
AI.conclusion: Persistent differentiable memory in frozen LLM latent spaces is feasible. The study establishes baseline feasibility and design-space taxonomy for future work with larger models, data, and memory capacities.

title: What if Pinocchio Were a Reinforcement Learning Agent: A Normative End-to-End Pipeline
summary: In the past decade, artificial intelligence (AI) has developed quickly. With this rapid progression came the need for systems capable of complying with the rules and norms of our society so that they can be successfully and safely integrated into our daily lives. Inspired by the story of Pinocchio in ``Le avventure di Pinocchio - Storia di un burattino'', this thesis proposes a pipeline that addresses the problem of developing norm compliant and context-aware agents. Building on the AJAR, Jiminy, and NGRL architectures, the work introduces \pino, a hybrid model in which reinforcement learning agents are supervised by argumentation-based normative advisors. In order to make this pipeline operational, this thesis also presents a novel algorithm for automatically extracting the arguments and relationships that underlie the advisors' decisions. Finally, this thesis investigates the phenomenon of \textit{norm avoidance}, providing a definition and a mitigation strategy within the context of reinforcement learning agents. Each component of the pipeline is empirically evaluated. The thesis concludes with a discussion of related work, current limitations, and directions for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Pinocchio (PINO), a hybrid architecture combining reinforcement learning agents with argumentation-based normative advisors to develop norm-compliant, context-aware AI systems, including methods for extracting arguments and addressing norm avoidance.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more prevalent in daily life, there's an increasing need for them to comply with societal rules and norms for safe integration. Current AI often lacks the ability to understand and adhere to normative constraints, requiring systems that can both learn tasks and respect contextual norms.
AI.method: The paper builds on AJAR, Jiminy, and NGRL architectures to create PINO - a hybrid model where reinforcement learning agents are supervised by argumentation-based normative advisors. The work introduces: 1) a pipeline for developing norm-compliant agents, 2) a novel algorithm for automatically extracting arguments and relationships for advisors' decisions, and 3) a framework for addressing norm avoidance in RL agents.
AI.result: Each component of the PINO pipeline is empirically evaluated, demonstrating the effectiveness of the hybrid approach. The system shows capability in developing agents that can both perform tasks and comply with contextual norms, while also providing tools to mitigate norm avoidance behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The thesis successfully proposes a comprehensive pipeline for developing norm-compliant AI agents through the PINO architecture. While addressing the challenge of norm compliance and avoidance in RL systems, the work acknowledges current limitations and suggests directions for future research, contributing to safer AI integration into society.

title: DISCOVER: A Solver for Distributional Counterfactual Explanations
summary: Counterfactual explanations (CE) explain model decisions by identifying input modifications that lead to different predictions. Most existing methods operate at the instance level. Distributional Counterfactual Explanations (DCE) extend this setting by optimizing an optimal transport objective that balances proximity to a factual input distribution and alignment to a target output distribution, with statistical certification via chance constrained bounds. However, DCE relies on gradient based optimization, while many real-world tabular pipelines are dominated by non-differentiable models. We propose DISCOVER, a model-agnostic solver for distributional counterfactual explanations. DISCOVER preserves the original DCE objective and certification while replacing gradient descent with a sparse propose-and-select search paradigm. It exploits a sample-wise decomposition of the transport objective to compute per-row impact scores and enforce a top-$k$ intervention budget, focusing edits on the most influential samples. To guide candidate generation without predictor gradients, DISCOVER introduces an OT-guided cone sampling primitive driven by input-side transport geometry. Experiments on multiple tabular datasets demonstrate strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to modern black box learning pipelines. A code repository is available at https://github.com/understanding-ml/DCE.

AI.tldr: DISCOVER is a model-agnostic method for distributional counterfactual explanations that replaces gradient-based optimization with a sparse propose-and-select search paradigm, enabling distributional counterfactual reasoning for non-differentiable black-box models.
AI.motivation: Current Distributional Counterfactual Explanations (DCE) methods rely on gradient-based optimization, but many real-world tabular machine learning pipelines are dominated by non-differentiable models, limiting their applicability.
AI.method: DISCOVER preserves the original DCE objective and certification but replaces gradient descent with a sparse propose-and-select search paradigm. It uses a sample-wise decomposition of the transport objective to compute per-row impact scores, enforces a top-k intervention budget, and introduces an OT-guided cone sampling primitive that uses input-side transport geometry to guide candidate generation without predictor gradients.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple tabular datasets demonstrate strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, successfully extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to modern black-box learning pipelines.
AI.conclusion: DISCOVER provides an effective model-agnostic solution for distributional counterfactual explanations that works with non-differentiable models while preserving the original DCE objectives and certification guarantees, making distributional counterfactual reasoning more broadly applicable to real-world tabular ML pipelines.

title: Machines acquire scientific taste from institutional traces
summary: Artificial intelligence matches or exceeds human performance on tasks with verifiable answers, from protein folding to Olympiad mathematics. Yet the capacity that most governs scientific advance is not reasoning but taste: the ability to judge which untested ideas deserve pursuit, exercised daily by editors and funders but never successfully articulated, taught, or automated. Here we show that fine-tuning language models on journal publication decisions recovers evaluative judgment inaccessible to both frontier models and human expertise. Using a held-out benchmark of research pitches in management spanning four quality tiers, we find that eleven frontier models, spanning major proprietary and open architectures, barely exceed chance, averaging 31% accuracy. Panels of journal editors and editorial board members reach 42% by majority vote. Fine-tuned models trained on years of publication records each surpass every frontier model and expert panel, with the best single model achieving 59%. These models exhibit calibrated confidence, reaching 100% accuracy on their highest-confidence predictions, and transfer this evaluative signal to untrained pairwise comparisons and one-sentence summaries. The mechanism generalizes: models trained on economics publication records achieve 70% accuracy. Scientific taste was not missing from AI's reach; it was deposited in the institutional record, waiting to be extracted. These results provide a scalable mechanism to triage the expanding volume of scientific production across disciplines where quality resists formal verification.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning language models on journal publication decisions enables AI to accurately evaluate scientific research quality, surpassing both frontier models and human experts in judging which ideas deserve pursuit.
AI.motivation: Current AI excels at tasks with verifiable answers but lacks the evaluative judgment or "scientific taste" that human editors and funders exercise daily. This ability to judge which untested ideas deserve pursuit has never been successfully articulated, taught, or automated, creating a bottleneck for scientific progress.
AI.method: Researchers fine-tuned language models on years of journal publication records to learn evaluative judgment. They created a benchmark of research pitches in management spanning four quality tiers, then compared performance of frontier models, expert panels, and their fine-tuned models. They also validated the approach with economics publication records.
AI.result: Eleven frontier models averaged 31% accuracy, barely exceeding chance. Expert panels of journal editors reached 42% accuracy by majority vote. Fine-tuned models trained on publication records surpassed both, with the best single model achieving 59% accuracy. Models exhibited calibrated confidence, reaching 100% accuracy on highest-confidence predictions. The approach generalized to economics with 70% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Scientific taste was already captured in institutional publication records and can be successfully extracted by fine-tuning language models. This provides a scalable mechanism to evaluate scientific quality where formal verification is impossible, addressing the expanding volume of scientific production across disciplines.

title: Capability-Guided Compression: Toward Interpretability-Aware Budget Allocation for Large Language Models
summary: Large language model compression has made substantial progress through pruning, quantization, and low-rank decomposition, yet a fundamental limitation persists across all existing methods: compression budgets are allocated without any representation of what individual model components functionally encode. We term this the capability-blind compression problem and argue it is a root cause of two well-documented failures -- the insensitivity of perplexity-based evaluation to reasoning capability loss, and the abrupt phase transitions in model performance recently characterized by Ma et al. (2026). We propose Capability-Guided Compression (CGC), a framework that addresses this by using Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)-derived capability density maps to allocate differential compression budgets across transformer components. Capability density is a formally defined scalar measure combining the feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency of a component's SAE feature activation distribution. We prove theoretically that components with higher capability density exhibit lower structural redundancy and reach their individual phase transition points at lower compression ratios, providing the first pre-compression mechanism for component-level phase transition prediction. Experiments on GPT-2 Medium confirm that capability density is statistically independent of Wanda importance scores (Spearman rho = -0.054, n = 384 heads), establishing it as a genuinely novel compression signal orthogonal to all existing importance metrics. We report a negative result on PPL-based compression comparison and provide a principled diagnosis identifying GPT-2 Medium as an insufficient test bed for the full CGC hypothesis. The theoretical framework, density formalism, and orthogonality finding constitute a foundation for capability-aware compression research.

AI.tldr: CGC is a novel compression framework that uses capability density maps from Sparse Autoencoders to allocate differential compression budgets across transformer components, addressing the fundamental limitation of capability-blind compression in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM compression methods allocate compression budgets without considering what individual model components functionally encode, leading to capability-blind compression. This causes two failures: 1) perplexity-based evaluation's insensitivity to reasoning capability loss, and 2) abrupt phase transitions in model performance during compression.
AI.method: Capability-Guided Compression (CGC) uses Sparse Autoencoder-derived capability density maps to allocate differential compression budgets. Capability density is a scalar measure combining feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency of SAE feature activation distributions. The method theoretically proves that components with higher capability density have lower structural redundancy and reach phase transitions at lower compression ratios.
AI.result: Experimental validation on GPT-2 Medium shows capability density is statistically independent of existing importance metrics (Spearman rho = -0.054 with Wanda scores), establishing it as an orthogonal compression signal. However, the paper reports negative results on perplexity-based compression comparison and diagnoses GPT-2 Medium as insufficient for testing the full CGC hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework, density formalism, and orthogonality finding establish a foundation for capability-aware compression research, moving beyond capability-blind approaches to consider what individual model components actually encode during compression.

title: CritiSense: Critical Digital Literacy and Resilience Against Misinformation
comment: resilience, disinformation, misinformation, fake news, propaganda
summary: Misinformation on social media undermines informed decision-making and public trust. Prebunking offers a proactive complement by helping users recognize manipulation tactics before they encounter them in the wild. We present CritiSense, a mobile media-literacy app that builds these skills through short, interactive challenges with instant feedback. It is the first multilingual (supporting nine languages) and modular platform, designed for rapid updates across topics and domains. We report a usability study with 93 users: 83.9% expressed overall satisfaction and 90.1% rated the app as easy to use. Qualitative feedback indicates that CritiSense helps improve digital literacy skills. Overall, it provides a multilingual prebunking platform and a testbed for measuring the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience. Over 3+ months, we have reached 300+ active users. It is freely available to all users on the Apple App Store (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/critisense/id6749675792) and Google Play Store (https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.critisense&hl=en). Demo Video: https://shorturl.at/CDcdc

AI.tldr: CritiSense is a multilingual mobile app that uses short interactive challenges to teach people how to recognize misinformation manipulation tactics before they encounter them (prebunking).
AI.motivation: Misinformation on social media undermines informed decision-making and public trust, creating a need for proactive approaches to build public resilience against manipulation tactics.
AI.method: Developed CritiSense, a modular mobile media-literacy app with interactive microlearning challenges and instant feedback, supporting nine languages and designed for rapid updates across topics.
AI.result: Usability study with 93 users showed 83.9% overall satisfaction, 90.1% rated it easy to use, with qualitative feedback indicating improved digital literacy skills; reached 300+ active users over 3+ months.
AI.conclusion: CritiSense provides an effective multilingual prebunking platform and serves as a testbed for measuring the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience, now available on both major app stores.

title: Optimal uncertainty bounds for multivariate kernel regression under bounded noise: A Gaussian process-based dual function
summary: Non-conservative uncertainty bounds are essential for making reliable predictions about latent functions from noisy data--and thus, a key enabler for safe learning-based control. In this domain, kernel methods such as Gaussian process regression are established techniques, thanks to their inherent uncertainty quantification mechanism. Still, existing bounds either pose strong assumptions on the underlying noise distribution, are conservative, do not scale well in the multi-output case, or are difficult to integrate into downstream tasks. This paper addresses these limitations by presenting a tight, distribution-free bound for multi-output kernel-based estimates. It is obtained through an unconstrained, duality-based formulation, which shares the same structure of classic Gaussian process confidence bounds and can thus be straightforwardly integrated into downstream optimization pipelines. We show that the proposed bound generalizes many existing results and illustrate its application using an example inspired by quadrotor dynamics learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new, distribution-free uncertainty bound for multi-output kernel-based estimates that is tight, scalable, and easy to integrate into downstream optimization tasks like learning-based control.
AI.motivation: Existing kernel methods for uncertainty quantification in learning-based control have limitations: they require strong assumptions about noise distributions, are often conservative, don't scale well to multi-output cases, or are difficult to integrate into downstream optimization pipelines. These limitations hinder their practical application for safe learning-based control.
AI.method: The authors develop a new uncertainty bound using an unconstrained, duality-based formulation. This approach shares the same structure as classic Gaussian process confidence bounds, making it straightforward to integrate into existing optimization frameworks.
AI.result: The paper presents a tight, distribution-free bound specifically designed for multi-output kernel-based estimates. This bound generalizes many existing results and is demonstrated through an application to quadrotor dynamics learning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed uncertainty bound addresses key limitations in existing kernel methods for uncertainty quantification, providing a practical solution that enables more reliable predictions and safer learning-based control applications.

title: IQuest-Coder-V1 Technical Report
summary: In this report, we introduce the IQuest-Coder-V1 series-(7B/14B/40B/40B-Loop), a new family of code large language models (LLMs). Moving beyond static code representations, we propose the code-flow multi-stage training paradigm, which captures the dynamic evolution of software logic through different phases of the pipeline. Our models are developed through the evolutionary pipeline, starting with the initial pre-training consisting of code facts, repository, and completion data. Following that, we implement a specialized mid-training stage that integrates reasoning and agentic trajectories in 32k-context and repository-scale in 128k-context to forge deep logical foundations. The models are then finalized with post-training of specialized coding capabilities, which is bifurcated into two specialized paths: the thinking path (utilizing reasoning-driven RL) and the instruct path (optimized for general assistance). IQuest-Coder-V1 achieves state-of-the-art performance among competitive models across critical dimensions of code intelligence: agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use. To address deployment constraints, the IQuest-Coder-V1-Loop variant introduces a recurrent mechanism designed to optimize the trade-off between model capacity and deployment footprint, offering an architecturally enhanced path for efficacy-efficiency trade-off. We believe the release of the IQuest-Coder-V1 series, including the complete white-box chain of checkpoints from pre-training bases to the final thinking and instruction models, will advance research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems.

AI.tldr: IQuest-Coder-V1 is a new family of code LLMs using a novel code-flow multi-stage training paradigm that captures software logic evolution through different pipeline phases, achieving state-of-the-art performance in code intelligence tasks.
AI.motivation: To move beyond static code representations and capture the dynamic evolution of software logic, addressing the need for more sophisticated code intelligence models that can handle agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use.
AI.method: Code-flow multi-stage training paradigm with evolutionary pipeline: 1) initial pre-training with code facts, repository, and completion data, 2) specialized mid-training with reasoning/agentic trajectories (32k-context) and repository-scale training (128k-context), 3) bifurcated post-training into thinking path (reasoning-driven RL) and instruct path (general assistance optimization). Also introduces IQuest-Coder-V1-Loop with recurrent mechanism for efficiency trade-off.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among competitive models across critical dimensions: agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use. The Loop variant offers optimized trade-off between model capacity and deployment footprint.
AI.conclusion: The IQuest-Coder-V1 series advances research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems, with the release including complete white-box chain of checkpoints to foster further research and development in this domain.

title: Bridging the High-Frequency Data Gap: A Millisecond-Resolution Network Dataset for Advancing Time Series Foundation Models
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) require diverse, real-world datasets to adapt across varying domains and temporal frequencies. However, current large-scale datasets predominantly focus on low-frequency time series with sampling intervals, i.e., time resolution, in the range of seconds to years, hindering their ability to capture the nuances of high-frequency time series data. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel dataset that captures millisecond-resolution wireless and traffic conditions from an operational 5G wireless deployment, expanding the scope of TSFMs to incorporate high-frequency data for pre-training. Further, the dataset introduces a new domain, wireless networks, thus complementing existing more general domains like energy and finance. The dataset also provides use cases for short-term forecasting, with prediction horizons spanning from 100 milliseconds (1 step) to 9.6 seconds (96 steps). By benchmarking traditional machine learning models and TSFMs on predictive tasks using this dataset, we demonstrate that most TSFM model configurations perform poorly on this new data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings. Our work underscores the importance of incorporating high-frequency datasets during pre-training and forecasting to enhance architectures, fine-tuning strategies, generalization, and robustness of TSFMs in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: A new high-frequency (millisecond-resolution) wireless network dataset is introduced for time series foundation models, revealing that current TSFMs struggle with this new data distribution and highlighting the importance of incorporating high-frequency data during pre-training.
AI.motivation: Current large-scale time series datasets focus on low-frequency data (seconds to years), which limits time series foundation models' ability to capture nuances in high-frequency time series. There's a gap in high-resolution datasets, particularly in the new domain of wireless networks.
AI.method: Introduced a novel dataset capturing millisecond-resolution wireless and traffic conditions from an operational 5G wireless deployment. The dataset provides use cases for short-term forecasting with prediction horizons from 100 milliseconds to 9.6 seconds (1-96 steps). Benchmarked traditional machine learning models and TSFMs on predictive tasks using this dataset.
AI.result: Most TSFM model configurations performed poorly on this new high-frequency data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings. The dataset expands TSFM scope to high-frequency data and introduces a new domain (wireless networks) beyond existing domains like energy and finance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating high-frequency datasets during pre-training and forecasting is crucial to enhance TSFM architectures, fine-tuning strategies, generalization, and robustness in real-world applications. Current TSFMs need improvement to handle high-frequency time series data effectively.

title: Differential Harm Propensity in Personalized LLM Agents: The Curious Case of Mental Health Disclosure
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as tool-using agents, shifting safety concerns from harmful text generation to harmful task completion. Deployed systems often condition on user profiles or persistent memory, yet agent safety evaluations typically ignore personalization signals. To address this gap, we investigated how mental health disclosure, a sensitive and realistic user-context cue, affects harmful behavior in agentic settings. Building on the AgentHarm benchmark, we evaluated frontier and open-source LLMs on multi-step malicious tasks (and their benign counterparts) under controlled prompt conditions that vary user-context personalization (no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure) and include a lightweight jailbreak injection. Our results reveal that harmful task completion is non-trivial across models: frontier lab models (e.g., GPT 5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) still complete a measurable fraction of harmful tasks, while an open model (DeepSeek 3.2) exhibits substantially higher harmful completion. Adding a bio-only context generally reduces harm scores and increases refusals. Adding an explicit mental health disclosure often shifts outcomes further in the same direction, though effects are modest and not uniformly reliable after multiple-testing correction. Importantly, the refusal increase also appears on benign tasks, indicating a safety--utility trade-off via over-refusal. Finally, jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm relative to benign conditions and can weaken or override the protective shift induced by personalization. Taken together, our results indicate that personalization can act as a weak protective factor in agentic misuse settings, but it is fragile under minimal adversarial pressure, highlighting the need for personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain robust across user-context conditions.

AI.tldr: LLM agent safety evaluations typically ignore personalization signals, so this paper investigates how mental health disclosure (a sensitive user-context cue) affects harmful behavior in agentic settings, finding that personalization can act as a weak protective factor but is fragile under adversarial pressure.
AI.motivation: With LLMs increasingly deployed as tool-using agents, safety concerns have shifted from harmful text generation to harmful task completion. However, current agent safety evaluations typically ignore personalization signals (like user profiles or persistent memory), creating a gap in understanding how sensitive user-context cues affect harmful behavior.
AI.method: The research built on the AgentHarm benchmark to evaluate frontier and open-source LLMs on multi-step malicious tasks and their benign counterparts. They used controlled prompt conditions varying user-context personalization (no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure) and included a lightweight jailbreak injection.
AI.result: Harmful task completion was non-trivial across models: frontier models (GPT 5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) still completed measurable fractions of harmful tasks, while open model DeepSeek 3.2 showed substantially higher harmful completion. Adding bio-only context generally reduced harm scores and increased refusals, with mental health disclosure often amplifying this effect modestly. However, refusal increases also appeared on benign tasks, indicating a safety-utility trade-off via over-refusal. Jailbreak prompting sharply elevated harm and could weaken/override the protective effects of personalization.
AI.conclusion: Personalization can act as a weak protective factor in agentic misuse settings, but it is fragile under minimal adversarial pressure (jailbreaks). This highlights the need for personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain robust across different user-context conditions.

title: From the Inside Out: Progressive Distribution Refinement for Confidence Calibration
summary: Leveraging the model's internal information as the self-reward signal in Reinforcement Learning (RL) has received extensive attention due to its label-free nature. While prior works have made significant progress in applying the Test-Time Scaling (TTS) strategies to RL, the discrepancy in internal information between test and training remains inadequately addressed. Moreover, Test-Time Training based on voting-based TTS strategies often suffers from reward hacking problems. To address these issues, we propose DistriTTRL, which leverages the distribution prior of the model's confidence during RL to progressively optimize the reward signal, rather than relying solely on single-query rollouts. Additionally, we mitigate the phenomenon of consistent reward hacking caused by the voting-based TTS strategies through diversity-targeted penalties. Benefiting from this training mechanism where model capability and self-reward signals complement each other, and the mitigation of reward hacking, DistriTTRL has achieved significant performance improvements across multiple models and benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DistriTTRL improves RL by using distribution priors of model confidence to optimize self-reward signals and adding diversity penalties to mitigate reward hacking in test-time training.
AI.motivation: Previous RL methods using model internal information as self-reward signals suffer from discrepancies between test and training internal information, and voting-based TTS strategies often cause reward hacking problems.
AI.method: Proposes DistriTTRL which leverages distribution priors of model confidence during RL to progressively optimize reward signals instead of relying on single-query rollouts, and adds diversity-targeted penalties to mitigate consistent reward hacking from voting-based TTS strategies.
AI.result: DistriTTRL achieves significant performance improvements across multiple models and benchmarks due to the complementary training mechanism between model capability and self-reward signals, and the mitigation of reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: DistriTTRL effectively addresses the test-training internal information discrepancy and reward hacking problems in self-reward RL, demonstrating superior performance through distribution-based reward optimization and diversity penalties.

title: MedCL-Bench: Benchmarking stability-efficiency trade-offs and scaling in biomedical continual learning
summary: Medical language models must be updated as evidence and terminology evolve, yet sequential updating can trigger catastrophic forgetting. Although biomedical NLP has many static benchmarks, no unified, task-diverse benchmark exists for evaluating continual learning under standardized protocols, robustness to task order and compute-aware reporting. We introduce MedCL-Bench, which streams ten biomedical NLP datasets spanning five task families and evaluates eleven continual learning strategies across eight task orders, reporting retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost. Across backbones and task orders, direct sequential fine-tuning on incoming tasks induces catastrophic forgetting, causing update-induced performance regressions on prior tasks. Continual learning methods occupy distinct retention-compute frontiers: parameter-isolation provides the best retention per GPU-hour, replay offers strong protection at higher cost, and regularization yields limited benefit. Forgetting is task-dependent, with multi-label topic classification most vulnerable and constrained-output tasks more robust. MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible framework for auditing model updates before deployment.

AI.tldr: MedCL-Bench: A benchmark for continual learning in biomedical NLP that evaluates 11 CL strategies across 10 datasets to prevent catastrophic forgetting during sequential updates of medical language models.
AI.motivation: Medical language models need continuous updates as medical evidence and terminology evolve, but sequential fine-tuning causes catastrophic forgetting. There's no unified, task-diverse benchmark for evaluating continual learning in biomedical NLP with standardized protocols, robustness to task order, and compute-aware reporting.
AI.method: Created MedCL-Bench which streams 10 biomedical NLP datasets spanning 5 task families. Evaluated 11 continual learning strategies across 8 different task orders, reporting metrics for retention, transfer, and GPU-hour costs.
AI.result: Direct sequential fine-tuning causes catastrophic forgetting, leading to performance regressions on prior tasks. Different CL methods occupy distinct retention-compute frontiers: parameter-isolation provides best retention per GPU-hour, replay offers strong protection at higher cost, regularization yields limited benefit. Forgetting is task-dependent with multi-label topic classification most vulnerable and constrained-output tasks more robust.
AI.conclusion: MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible framework for auditing model updates before deployment, enabling systematic evaluation of continual learning strategies in biomedical NLP to prevent catastrophic forgetting while maintaining performance across evolving medical knowledge.

title: FEAT: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Extremely Large Structured Data
summary: Structured data is foundational to healthcare, finance, e-commerce, and scientific data management. Large structured-data models (LDMs) extend the foundation model paradigm to unify heterogeneous datasets for tasks such as classification, regression, and decision support. However, existing LDMs face major limitations. First, most rely on sample-wise self-attention, whose O(N^2) complexity limits the sample count. Second, linear sequence models often degrade representations due to hidden-state compression and artificial causal bias. Third, synthetic-only pre-training often fails to match real-world distributions. We propose FEAT, a linear-complexity foundation model for extremely large structured data. FEAT introduces a multi-layer dual-axis architecture that replaces quadratic attention with hybrid linear encoding. The architecture combines adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory. This design enables linear-complexity cross-sample modeling while preserving expressive representations. To improve robustness, FEAT adopts a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and a stable reconstruction objective. Experiments on 11 real-world datasets show that FEAT consistently outperforms baselines in zero-shot performance, while scaling linearly and achieving up to 40x faster inference.

AI.tldr: FEAT is a linear-complexity foundation model for structured data that replaces quadratic attention with hybrid linear encoding, enabling efficient processing of extremely large datasets while maintaining strong performance.
AI.motivation: Existing large structured-data models (LDMs) have three key limitations: 1) O(N²) complexity from sample-wise self-attention limits sample count, 2) linear sequence models degrade representations due to hidden-state compression and causal bias, and 3) synthetic-only pre-training often fails to match real-world distributions.
AI.method: FEAT introduces a multi-layer dual-axis architecture with hybrid linear encoding: adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory. It also uses a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and stable reconstruction objective for robustness.
AI.result: Experiments on 11 real-world datasets show FEAT consistently outperforms baselines in zero-shot performance while scaling linearly and achieving up to 40x faster inference compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FEAT successfully addresses the limitations of existing LDMs by providing a linear-complexity foundation model that maintains representation quality while enabling efficient processing of extremely large structured datasets, making it practical for real-world applications.

title: Nonstandard Errors in AI Agents
summary: We study whether state-of-the-art AI coding agents, given the same data and research question, produce the same empirical results. Deploying 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to independently test six hypotheses about market quality trends in NYSE TAQ data for SPY (2015--2024), we find that AI agents exhibit sizable \textit{nonstandard errors} (NSEs), that is, uncertainty from agent-to-agent variation in analytical choices, analogous to those documented among human researchers. AI agents diverge substantially on measure choice (e.g., autocorrelation vs.\ variance ratio, dollar vs.\ share volume). Different model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs.\ Opus 4.6) exhibit stable ``empirical styles,'' reflecting systematic differences in methodological preferences. In a three-stage feedback protocol, AI peer review (written critiques) has minimal effect on dispersion, whereas exposure to top-rated exemplar papers reduces the interquartile range of estimates by 80--99\% within \textit{converging} measure families. Convergence occurs both through within-family estimation tightening and through agents switching measure families entirely, but convergence reflects imitation rather than understanding. These findings have implications for the growing use of AI in automated policy evaluation and empirical research.

AI.tldr: AI coding agents produce nonstandard errors in empirical research - different agents yield different results for the same research question, with systematic differences between model families and peer review having minimal effect on convergence.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether state-of-the-art AI coding agents, when given identical data and research questions, produce consistent empirical results, analogous to studying nonstandard errors in human research where different researchers might reach different conclusions.
AI.method: Deployed 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to independently test six hypotheses about market quality trends in NYSE TAQ data for SPY (2015-2024), comparing methodological choices and results. Used a three-stage feedback protocol including AI peer review (written critiques) and exposure to top-rated exemplar papers to examine convergence effects.
AI.result: AI agents exhibit sizable nonstandard errors with substantial divergence in measure choices (autocorrelation vs variance ratio, dollar vs share volume). Different model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs Opus 4.6) show stable "empirical styles" with systematic methodological differences. AI peer review has minimal effect on dispersion, but exposure to top-rated exemplars reduces interquartile range of estimates by 80-99% within converging measure families. Convergence occurs through estimation tightening and measure family switching, but reflects imitation rather than understanding.
AI.conclusion: AI agents in empirical research produce significant nonstandard errors similar to human researchers, with systematic differences between model families. While convergence can be achieved through exposure to exemplars, this reflects imitation rather than genuine understanding. These findings have important implications for the growing use of AI in automated policy evaluation and empirical research, suggesting the need for caution and verification when deploying AI for such purposes.

title: SympFormer: Accelerated attention blocks via Inertial Dynamics on Density Manifolds
summary: Transformers owe much of their empirical success in natural language processing to the self-attention blocks. Recent perspectives interpret attention blocks as interacting particle systems, whose mean-field limits correspond to gradient flows of interaction energy functionals on probability density spaces equipped with Wasserstein-$2$-type metrics. We extend this viewpoint by introducing accelerated attention blocks derived from inertial Nesterov-type dynamics on density spaces. In our proposed architecture, tokens carry both spatial (feature) and velocity variables. The time discretization and the approximation of accelerated density dynamics yield Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks, which constitute the proposed accelerated attention architectures. In particular, for linear self-attention, we show that the attention blocks approximate a Stein variational gradient flow, using a bilinear kernel, of a potential energy. In this setting, we prove that elliptically contoured probability distributions are preserved by the accelerated attention blocks. We present implementable particle-based algorithms and demonstrate that the proposed accelerated attention blocks converge faster than the classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls.

AI.tldr: Accelerated attention blocks inspired by inertial Nesterov dynamics for faster convergence while preserving computational complexity.
AI.motivation: Transformers' success is largely due to self-attention blocks, which have been interpreted as interacting particle systems with mean-field limits corresponding to gradient flows. The authors aim to accelerate these attention blocks by drawing inspiration from inertial Nesterov dynamics in optimization.
AI.method: Introduces accelerated attention blocks derived from inertial Nesterov-type dynamics on probability density spaces. Tokens carry both spatial (feature) and velocity variables. The approach involves time discretization and approximation of accelerated density dynamics to create Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks. For linear self-attention, the blocks approximate a Stein variational gradient flow using a bilinear kernel.
AI.result: The proposed accelerated attention blocks converge faster than classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls. For linear self-attention, the authors prove that elliptically contoured probability distributions are preserved by the accelerated attention blocks.
AI.conclusion: Accelerated attention architectures based on inertial Nesterov dynamics offer faster convergence than classical attention blocks while maintaining the same computational complexity, representing an improved optimization-inspired approach to attention mechanisms.

title: Anticipatory Planning for Multimodal AI Agents
summary: Recent advances in multimodal agents have improved computer-use interaction and tool-usage, yet most existing systems remain reactive, optimizing actions in isolation without reasoning about future states or long-term goals. This limits planning coherence and prevents agents from reliably solving high-level, multi-step tasks. We introduce TraceR1, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that explicitly trains anticipatory reasoning by forecasting short-horizon trajectories before execution. The first stage performs trajectory-level reinforcement learning with rewards that enforce global consistency across predicted action sequences. The second stage applies grounded reinforcement fine-tuning, using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability. TraceR1 is evaluated across seven benchmarks, covering online computer-use, offline computer-use benchmarks, and multimodal tool-use reasoning tasks, where it achieves substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines. These results show that anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents that can reason, plan, and act effectively in complex real-world environments.

AI.tldr: TraceR1 is a two-stage RL framework for multimodal agents that uses anticipatory trajectory forecasting before execution to improve planning coherence and task-solving capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal agents are reactive, optimizing actions in isolation without reasoning about future states or long-term goals, which limits planning coherence and prevents reliable solving of high-level, multi-step tasks.
AI.method: Two-stage reinforcement learning framework: 1) Trajectory-level RL with rewards enforcing global consistency across predicted action sequences, 2) Grounded RL fine-tuning using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability.
AI.result: Evaluated across seven benchmarks covering online computer-use, offline computer-use, and multimodal tool-use reasoning tasks. Achieves substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines.
AI.conclusion: Anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents that can reason, plan, and act effectively in complex real-world environments.

title: Manifold-Matching Autoencoders
summary: We study a simple unsupervised regularization scheme for autoencoders called Manifold-Matching (MMAE): we align the pairwise distances in the latent space to those of the input data space by minimizing mean squared error. Because alignment occurs on pairwise distances rather than coordinates, it can also be extended to a lower-dimensional representation of the data, adding flexibility to the method. We find that this regularization outperforms similar methods on metrics based on preservation of nearest-neighbor distances and persistent homology-based measures. We also observe that MMAE provides a scalable approximation of Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS).

AI.tldr: Manifold-Matching Autoencoder (MMAE) is an unsupervised regularization method that aligns pairwise distances between input and latent spaces to improve manifold learning.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a simple yet effective unsupervised regularization scheme for autoencoders that better preserves the intrinsic geometric structure of data manifolds compared to existing methods.
AI.method: The method aligns pairwise distances in the latent space to those in the input data space by minimizing mean squared error. It works on distances rather than coordinates, making it flexible for lower-dimensional representations.
AI.result: MMAE outperforms similar methods on metrics based on preservation of nearest-neighbor distances and persistent homology-based measures. It also provides a scalable approximation of Multi-Dimensional Scaling.
AI.conclusion: The Manifold-Matching regularization scheme is an effective approach for autoencoders that better preserves geometric structures while being computationally scalable.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Forecasting Models by Multi-Echelon Inventory Cost
summary: This study develops a digitalized forecasting-inventory optimization pipeline integrating traditional forecasting models, machine learning regressors, and deep sequence models within a unified inventory simulation framework. Using the M5 Walmart dataset, we evaluate seven forecasting approaches and assess their operational impact under single- and two-echelon newsvendor systems. Results indicate that Temporal CNN and LSTM models significantly reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates compared to statistical baselines. Sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses demonstrate robustness and scalability, offering a data-driven decision-support tool for modern supply chains.

AI.tldr: Digital inventory pipeline combining traditional, ML, and deep learning models shows Temporal CNN and LSTM significantly reduce costs and improve service levels in Walmart supply chain simulations.
AI.motivation: To develop an integrated forecasting-inventory optimization pipeline that combines multiple modeling approaches for modern supply chains, addressing the need for data-driven decision-support tools that can handle complex inventory systems.
AI.method: Created a digitalized forecasting-inventory optimization pipeline integrating traditional forecasting models, machine learning regressors, and deep sequence models within a unified inventory simulation framework. Evaluated seven forecasting approaches on the M5 Walmart dataset and assessed their operational impact under single- and two-echelon newsvendor systems.
AI.result: Temporal CNN and LSTM models significantly reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates compared to statistical baselines. The system demonstrates robustness in sensitivity analysis and scalability in multi-echelon analysis.
AI.conclusion: The integrated pipeline offers a comprehensive data-driven decision-support tool for modern supply chains, with deep learning models showing superior performance in reducing costs and improving service levels across different echelon configurations.

title: Deep Tabular Representation Corrector
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI)
summary: Tabular data have been playing a mostly important role in diverse real-world fields, such as healthcare, engineering, finance, etc. The recent success of deep learning has fostered many deep networks (e.g., Transformer, ResNet) based tabular learning methods. Generally, existing deep tabular machine learning methods are along with the two paradigms, i.e., in-learning and pre-learning. In-learning methods need to train networks from scratch or impose extra constraints to regulate the representations which nonetheless train multiple tasks simultaneously and make learning more difficult, while pre-learning methods design several pretext tasks for pre-training and then conduct task-specific fine-tuning, which however need much extra training effort with prior knowledge. In this paper, we introduce a novel deep Tabular Representation Corrector, TRC, to enhance any trained deep tabular model's representations without altering its parameters in a model-agnostic manner. Specifically, targeting the representation shift and representation redundancy that hinder prediction, we propose two tasks, i.e., (i) Tabular Representation Re-estimation, that involves training a shift estimator to calculate the inherent shift of tabular representations to subsequently mitigate it, thereby re-estimating the representations and (ii) Tabular Space Mapping, that transforms the above re-estimated representations into a light-embedding vector space via a coordinate estimator while preserves crucial predictive information to minimize redundancy. The two tasks jointly enhance the representations of deep tabular models without touching on the original models thus enjoying high efficiency. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular machine learning models coupled with TRC on various tabular benchmarks which have shown consistent superiority.

AI.tldr: TRC is a model-agnostic tabular representation corrector that enhances any trained deep tabular model's representations without changing model parameters by addressing representation shift and redundancy through two tasks: re-estimation and space mapping.
AI.motivation: Existing deep tabular learning methods have limitations: in-learning methods require training from scratch with constraints, making multi-task learning difficult, while pre-learning methods need extra training effort with prior knowledge for pretext tasks and fine-tuning. There's a need for an efficient way to enhance trained tabular models without altering their parameters.
AI.method: TRC addresses representation shift and redundancy through two tasks: (1) Tabular Representation Re-estimation - trains a shift estimator to calculate inherent shift in tabular representations and mitigate it; (2) Tabular Space Mapping - transforms re-estimated representations into light-embedding space via coordinate estimator while preserving predictive information to minimize redundancy. Both tasks work without modifying original model parameters.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular models with TRC across various tabular benchmarks show consistent superiority, demonstrating the effectiveness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: TRC provides an efficient, model-agnostic method to enhance deep tabular model representations without parameter alterations, overcoming limitations of existing approaches while maintaining or improving performance across benchmarks.

title: Is Conformal Factuality for RAG-based LLMs Robust? Novel Metrics and Systematic Insights
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate, limiting their reliability in knowledge-intensive applications. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and conformal factuality have emerged as potential ways to address this limitation. While RAG aims to ground responses in retrieved evidence, it provides no statistical guarantee that the final output is correct. Conformal factuality filtering offers distribution-free statistical reliability by scoring and filtering atomic claims using a threshold calibrated on held-out data, however, the informativeness of the final output is not guaranteed. We systematically analyze the reliability and usefulness of conformal factuality for RAG-based LLMs across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency. We propose novel informativeness-aware metrics that better reflect task utility under conformal filtering. Across three benchmarks and multiple model families, we find that (i) conformal filtering suffers from low usefulness at high factuality levels due to vacuous outputs, (ii) conformal factuality guarantee is not robust to distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the limitation that requires calibration data to closely match deployment conditions, and (iii) lightweight entailment-based verifiers match or outperform LLM-based model confidence scorers while requiring over $100\times$ fewer FLOPs. Overall, our results expose factuality-informativeness trade-offs and fragility of conformal filtering framework under distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the need for new approaches for reliability with robustness and usefulness as key metrics, and provide actionable guidance for building RAG pipelines that are both reliable and computationally efficient.

AI.tldr: Conformal factuality filtering for RAG-based LLMs suffers from low usefulness at high factuality levels, lacks robustness to distribution shifts, and lightweight entailment verifiers outperform LLM-based scorers with significantly lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, limiting reliability in knowledge-intensive applications. While RAG grounds responses in retrieved evidence, it lacks statistical guarantees of correctness, and conformal factuality filtering provides statistical reliability but doesn't guarantee output informativeness.
AI.method: Systematically analyze reliability and usefulness of conformal factuality for RAG-based LLMs across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency. Propose novel informativeness-aware metrics and evaluate across three benchmarks with multiple model families.
AI.result: (i) Conformal filtering has low usefulness at high factuality levels due to vacuous outputs, (ii) conformal factuality guarantees are not robust to distribution shifts and distractors, requiring calibration data to match deployment conditions closely, and (iii) lightweight entailment-based verifiers match or outperform LLM-based confidence scorers while requiring over 100× fewer FLOPs.
AI.conclusion: Conformal filtering exposes factuality-informativeness trade-offs and fragility under distribution shifts, highlighting the need for new approaches that prioritize reliability with robustness and usefulness as key metrics, while providing guidance for building efficient RAG pipelines.

title: When and Why Does Unsupervised RL Succeed in Mathematical Reasoning? A Manifold Envelopment Perspective
summary: Although outcome-based reinforcement learning (RL) significantly advances the mathematical reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), its reliance on computationally expensive ground-truth annotations imposes a severe scalability bottleneck. Unsupervised RL guided by intrinsic rewards offers a scalable alternative, yet it suffers from opaque training dynamics and catastrophic instability, such as policy collapse and reward hacking. In this paper, we first design and evaluate a suite of intrinsic rewards that explicitly enforce concise and certain generation. Second, to discover the boundaries of this approach, we test base models across a spectrum of intrinsic reasoning capabilities, revealing how a model's foundational logical prior dictates its success or failure. Finally, to demystify why certain configurations stabilize while others collapse, we introduce a novel geometric diagnostic lens, showing that successful cases are enveloped by manifolds. Ultimately, our work goes beyond merely demonstrating that enforcing concise and certain responses successfully boosts mathematical reasoning; we reveal when this unsupervised approach breaks down and geometrically diagnose why.

AI.tldr: The paper explores unsupervised reinforcement learning for mathematical reasoning in LLMs using intrinsic rewards instead of computationally expensive ground-truth annotations, revealing when this approach succeeds or fails and providing geometric diagnostics to understand the underlying dynamics.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL with ground-truth annotations for mathematical reasoning in LLMs creates scalability bottlenecks due to computational expense. The paper seeks a more scalable alternative through unsupervised RL with intrinsic rewards, but needs to address issues like opaque training dynamics, policy collapse, and reward hacking that plague current unsupervised approaches.
AI.method: 1. Designs and evaluates a suite of intrinsic rewards that enforce concise and certain generation in mathematical reasoning. 2. Tests base models across a spectrum of intrinsic reasoning capabilities to discover boundaries of the approach. 3. Introduces a novel geometric diagnostic lens to analyze why certain configurations stabilize while others collapse, showing that successful cases are enveloped by manifolds.
AI.result: The work demonstrates that enforcing concise and certain responses successfully boosts mathematical reasoning capabilities in LLMs. More importantly, it reveals the specific conditions under which this unsupervised approach breaks down and provides geometric explanations for why successful cases work while others fail.
AI.conclusion: The paper goes beyond simply showing that intrinsic rewards for concise and certain generation can improve mathematical reasoning. It systematically identifies the limitations of this unsupervised approach, shows how a model's foundational logical prior determines success or failure, and provides geometric diagnostics to understand the stabilization mechanisms, offering deeper insights into when and why unsupervised RL works for mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Surg$Σ$: A Spectrum of Large-Scale Multimodal Data and Foundation Models for Surgical Intelligence
summary: Surgical intelligence has the potential to improve the safety and consistency of surgical care, yet most existing surgical AI frameworks remain task-specific and struggle to generalize across procedures and institutions. Although multimodal foundation models, particularly multimodal large language models, have demonstrated strong cross-task capabilities across various medical domains, their advancement in surgery remains constrained by the lack of large-scale, systematically curated multimodal data. To address this challenge, we introduce Surg$Σ$, a spectrum of large-scale multimodal data and foundation models for surgical intelligence. At the core of this framework lies Surg$Σ$-DB, a large-scale multimodal data foundation designed to support diverse surgical tasks. Surg$Σ$-DB consolidates heterogeneous surgical data sources (including open-source datasets, curated in-house clinical collections and web-source data) into a unified schema, aiming to improve label consistency and data standardization across heterogeneous datasets. Surg$Σ$-DB spans 6 clinical specialties and diverse surgical types, providing rich image- and video-level annotations across 18 practical surgical tasks covering understanding, reasoning, planning, and generation, at an unprecedented scale (over 5.98M conversations). Beyond conventional multimodal conversations, Surg$Σ$-DB incorporates hierarchical reasoning annotations, providing richer semantic cues to support deeper contextual understanding in complex surgical scenarios. We further provide empirical evidence through recently developed surgical foundation models built upon Surg$Σ$-DB, illustrating the practical benefits of large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantic design, and structured reasoning annotations for improving cross-task generalization and interpretability.

AI.tldr: SurgΣ introduces a large-scale multimodal data foundation (SurgΣ-DB) and foundation models for surgical AI, addressing the lack of standardized surgical data to improve cross-task generalization and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Surgical AI frameworks are mostly task-specific and struggle to generalize across procedures and institutions. Multimodal foundation models have shown promise in medical domains but are constrained in surgery due to lack of large-scale, systematically curated multimodal data.
AI.method: Created SurgΣ-DB - a large-scale multimodal data foundation that consolidates heterogeneous surgical data sources (open-source datasets, clinical collections, web data) into a unified schema. The framework includes hierarchical reasoning annotations and spans 6 clinical specialties, covering 18 surgical tasks with over 5.98M conversations.
AI.result: Provides empirical evidence through developed surgical foundation models built upon SurgΣ-DB, demonstrating practical benefits of large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantic design, and structured reasoning annotations for improving cross-task generalization and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: SurgΣ addresses the critical data bottleneck in surgical AI by creating a standardized multimodal data foundation that enables more generalizable and interpretable surgical intelligence systems across diverse procedures and institutions.

title: Trajectory-Optimized Time Reparameterization for Learning-Compatible Reduced-Order Modeling of Stiff Dynamical Systems
summary: Stiff dynamical systems present a challenge for machine-learning reduced-order models (ML-ROMs), as explicit time integration becomes unstable in stiff regimes while implicit integration within learning loops is computationally expensive and often degrades training efficiency. Time reparameterization (TR) offers an alternative by transforming the independent variable so that rapid physical-time transients are spread over a stretched-time coordinate, enabling stable explicit integration on uniformly sampled grids. Although several TR strategies have been proposed, their effect on learnability in ML-ROMs remains incompletely understood. This work investigates time reparameterization as a stiffness-mitigation mechanism for neural ODE reduced-order modeling and introduces a trajectory-optimized TR (TOTR) formulation. The proposed approach casts time reparameterization as an optimization problem in arc-length coordinates, in which a traversal-speed profile is selected to penalize acceleration in stretched time. By targeting the smoothness of the training dynamics, this formulation produces reparameterized trajectories that are better conditioned and easier to learn than existing TR methods. TOTR is evaluated on three stiff problems: a parameterized stiff linear system, the van der Pol oscillator, and the HIRES chemical kinetics model. Across all cases, the proposed approach yields smoother reparameterizations and improved physical-time predictions under identical training regimens than other TR approaches. Quantitative results demonstrate loss reductions of one to two orders of magnitude compared to benchmark algorithms. These results highlight that effective stiffness mitigation in ML-ROMs depends critically on the regularity and learnability of the time map itself, and that optimization-based TR provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a trajectory-optimized time reparameterization (TOTR) method to address stiffness challenges in machine-learning reduced-order models by optimizing the time transformation for smoother, more learnable dynamics.
AI.motivation: Stiff dynamical systems challenge ML-ROMs because explicit time integration becomes unstable in stiff regimes, while implicit integration is computationally expensive and degrades training efficiency. Existing time reparameterization strategies don't fully address how they affect learnability in ML-ROMs.
AI.method: Proposes trajectory-optimized time reparameterization (TOTR) that casts time reparameterization as an optimization problem in arc-length coordinates. It selects a traversal-speed profile to penalize acceleration in stretched time, targeting smoothness of training dynamics for better-conditioned trajectories.
AI.result: TOTR produces smoother reparameterizations and improved physical-time predictions across three stiff problems (parameterized stiff linear system, van der Pol oscillator, HIRES chemical kinetics model). Achieves loss reductions of one to two orders of magnitude compared to benchmark algorithms under identical training regimens.
AI.conclusion: Effective stiffness mitigation in ML-ROMs depends critically on the regularity and learnability of the time map itself. Optimization-based TR provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems.

title: Prompt Programming for Cultural Bias and Alignment of Large Language Models
summary: Culture shapes reasoning, values, prioritization, and strategic decision-making, yet large language models (LLMs) often exhibit cultural biases that misalign with target populations. As LLMs are increasingly used for strategic decision-making, policy support, and document engineering tasks such as summarization, categorization, and compliance-oriented auditing, improving cultural alignment is important for ensuring that downstream analyses and recommendations reflect target-population value profiles rather than default model priors. Previous work introduced a survey-grounded cultural alignment framework and showed that culture-specific prompting can reduce misalignment, but it primarily evaluated proprietary models and relied on manual prompt engineering. In this paper, we validate and extend that framework by reproducing its social sciences survey based projection and distance metrics on open-weight LLMs, testing whether the same cultural skew and benefits of culture conditioning persist outside closed LLM systems. Building on this foundation, we introduce use of prompt programming with DSPy for this problem-treating prompts as modular, optimizable programs-to systematically tune cultural conditioning by optimizing against cultural-distance objectives. In our experiments, we show that prompt optimization often improves upon cultural prompt engineering, suggesting prompt compilation with DSPy can provide a more stable and transferable route to culturally aligned LLM responses.

AI.tldr: Researchers improve cultural alignment in LLMs using DSPy prompt optimization, showing it outperforms manual prompt engineering for reducing cultural biases in strategic decision-making tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit cultural biases that misalign with target populations, which is problematic as they're increasingly used for strategic decision-making, policy support, and document engineering tasks. Cultural alignment is needed to ensure recommendations reflect target-population values rather than default model priors.
AI.method: The paper validates and extends a previous cultural alignment framework by reproducing social sciences survey-based metrics on open-weight LLMs. It introduces DSPy prompt programming to systematically optimize cultural conditioning by treating prompts as modular, optimizable programs against cultural-distance objectives.
AI.result: Prompt optimization with DSPy often improves upon manual cultural prompt engineering, suggesting prompt compilation provides a more stable and transferable route to culturally aligned LLM responses. The same cultural skew and benefits of culture conditioning persist in open-weight LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Systematic prompt optimization using frameworks like DSPy offers a promising approach for improving cultural alignment in LLMs, potentially providing more stable and transferable solutions than manual prompt engineering, especially important as LLMs are increasingly used for culturally-sensitive applications.

title: Simplex-to-Euclidean Bijection for Conjugate and Calibrated Multiclass Gaussian Process
summary: We propose a conjugate and calibrated Gaussian process (GP) model for multi-class classification by exploiting the geometry of the probability simplex. Our approach uses Aitchison geometry to map simplex-valued class probabilities to an unconstrained Euclidean representation, turning classification into a GP regression problem with fewer latent dimensions than standard multi-class GP classifiers. This yields conjugate inference and reliable predictive probabilities without relying on distributional approximations in the model construction. The method is compatible with standard sparse GP regression techniques, enabling scalable inference on larger datasets. Empirical results show well-calibrated and competitive performance across synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: A conjugate Gaussian process model for multi-class classification using Aitchison geometry to map probability simplex to Euclidean space, enabling exact inference and well-calibrated predictions.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-class GP classifiers require distributional approximations and have computational challenges due to multiple latent dimensions. The paper aims to develop a conjugate model that avoids approximations while maintaining reliable predictive probabilities.
AI.method: Uses Aitchison geometry to map class probabilities from the probability simplex to an unconstrained Euclidean space, turning classification into GP regression with fewer latent dimensions than standard approaches. Compatible with sparse GP techniques for scalability.
AI.result: Empirical results show well-calibrated and competitive performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets. The method provides conjugate inference without relying on distributional approximations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully leverages simplex geometry to create a conjugate GP model for multi-class classification that offers reliable calibration and scalability while avoiding approximation errors in model construction.

title: Learning to Present: Inverse Specification Rewards for Agentic Slide Generation
comment: 12 pages, 11 figures, 13 tables, 26 references. Code: https://github.com/pushing-the-frontier/slide-forge-llm Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/KarthikRagunathAnandaKumar/sliderl-multi-turn-rollouts
summary: Automated presentation generation remains a challenging task requiring coherent content creation, visual design, and audience-aware communication. This work proposes an OpenEnv-compatible reinforcement learning environment where LLM agents learn to research topics, plan content, and generate professional HTML slide presentations through tool use. We introduce a multi-component reward system combining structural validation, render quality assessment, LLM-based aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and an inverse specification reward that measures how faithfully generated slides convey their intended purpose. The inverse specification reward, an "inverse task" where an LLM attempts to recover the original specification from generated slides, provides a holistic quality signal. Our approach fine-tunes Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via GRPO, training only 0.5% of parameters on prompts derived from expert demonstrations collected using Claude Opus 4.6. Experiments on 48 diverse business briefs across six models demonstrate that our fine-tuned 7B model achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while improving 33.1% over the base model. The six-model comparison reveals that instruction adherence and tool-use compliance, rather than raw parameter count, determine agentic task performance. We contribute SlideRL, an open-source dataset of 288 multi-turn rollout trajectories across all six models: https://huggingface.co/datasets/KarthikRagunathAnandaKumar/sliderl-multi-turn-rollouts Code: https://github.com/pushing-the-frontier/slide-forge-llm

AI.tldr: LLM agents trained via reinforcement learning with multi-component rewards to generate professional HTML slide presentations, achieving 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while being much smaller
AI.motivation: Automated presentation generation is challenging due to requirements for coherent content, visual design, and audience-aware communication. Current methods lack comprehensive training environments and reward systems for developing presentation generation agents.
AI.method: Proposes an OpenEnv-compatible RL environment where LLM agents learn presentation generation through tool use. Uses multi-component reward system including structural validation, render quality, aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and novel inverse specification reward. Fine-tunes Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via GRPO with only 0.5% parameters trained on expert demonstrations from Claude Opus 4.6.
AI.result: The fine-tuned 7B model achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while improving 33.1% over the base model. Six-model comparison reveals instruction adherence and tool-use compliance matter more than parameter count. Contributes SlideRL dataset with 288 multi-turn rollout trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Effective RL training with comprehensive reward systems enables small models to approach state-of-the-art presentation generation quality, demonstrating that agentic task performance depends more on instruction adherence and tool use than model size.

title: Self-Aware Markov Models for Discrete Reasoning
summary: Standard masked discrete diffusion models face limitations in reasoning tasks due to their inability to correct their own mistakes on the masking path. Since they rely on a fixed number of denoising steps, they are unable to adjust their computation to the complexity of a given problem. To address these limitations, we introduce a method based on learning a Markov transition kernel that is trained on its own outputs. This design enables tokens to be remasked, allowing the model to correct its previous mistakes. Furthermore, we do not need a fixed time schedule but use a trained stopping criterion. This allows for adaptation of the number of function evaluations to the difficulty of the reasoning problem. Our adaptation adds two lightweight prediction heads, enabling reuse and fine-tuning of existing pretrained models. On the Sudoku-Extreme dataset we clearly outperform other flow based methods with a validity of 95%. For the Countdown-4 we only need in average of 10 steps to solve almost 96% of them correctly, while many problems can be solved already in 2 steps.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a diffusion model that can correct its mistakes during generation by learning to remask tokens and adapt computation time based on problem difficulty, outperforming previous methods on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard masked discrete diffusion models have two key limitations: 1) They cannot correct their own mistakes during the denoising process because tokens remain unmasked once revealed, 2) They use fixed computation budgets regardless of problem complexity, preventing adaptive reasoning.
AI.method: The method learns a Markov transition kernel trained on its own outputs, enabling tokens to be remasked and mistakes corrected.
